Daniel Goleman's brilliant report from the frontiers of psychology and neuroscience offers startling new insight into our "two minds"—the rational and the emotional—and how they together shape our destiny.

entries.title: Outliers: The Story of Success
entries.author: Gladwell, Malcolm
entries.abstract: In this stunning new book, Malcolm Gladwell takes us on an intellectual journey through the world of "outliers"—the best and the brightest, the most famous and the most successful. He asks the question: what makes high-achievers different? His answer is that we pay too much attention to what successful people are like, and too little attention to where they are from: that is, their culture, their family, their generation, and the idiosyncratic experiences of their upbringing.

entries.title: The AI Advantage: How to Put the Artificial Intelligence Revolution to Work
entries.author: Davenport, Thomas H.
entries.abstract: This pragmatic guide examines how organizations can implement AI to augment human capabilities rather than replace workers. Davenport introduces a taxonomy of AI applications: robotic process automation (automating structured tasks), cognitive insight (pattern detection in large datasets), and cognitive engagement (intelligent agents that interact with humans). Through case studies from finance, healthcare, media, and manufacturing, the book demonstrates that the most successful AI deployments enhance human decision-making rather than attempting full autonomy. Key recommendations include starting with existing business processes, combining AI with human judgment, and treating AI as a portfolio of capabilities rather than a single technology. For hummbl-dev, Davenport's augmentation thesis validates the Morning Briefing architecture: the system does not make decisions for the founder but synthesizes information from seven adapters into a decision-support artifact that amplifies the founder's pattern recognition and reduces information overload.

entries.title: Prediction Machines: The Simple Economics of Artificial Intelligence
entries.author: Agrawal, Ajay and Gans, Joshua and Goldfarb, Avi
entries.abstract: This book reframes artificial intelligence as a technology that dramatically reduces the cost of prediction, and then uses economic theory to analyze the cascading effects of cheap prediction on business strategy, organizational design, and competitive dynamics. The authors argue that as prediction becomes cheaper, the complementary inputs -- judgment, data, and action -- become more valuable. They introduce a decision-making framework decomposing decisions into prediction (what will happen), judgment (what matters), action (executing the choice), and outcome (learning from results). The book demonstrates how AI shifts the optimal boundaries of firms, changes the value of human judgment, and creates new strategic trade-offs between speed and accuracy. For hummbl-dev, this framework directly justifies the architecture: the briefing system automates prediction (synthesizing signals from GitHub, Linear, Calendar, cost tracking), while human judgment remains the scarce complement that the founder applies to the Morning Briefing's recommendations.

entries.title: The Five Dysfunctions of a Team: A Leadership Fable
entries.author: Lencioni, Patrick
entries.abstract: This influential leadership fable presents a hierarchical model of five interconnected dysfunctions that undermine team effectiveness: absence of trust (fear of vulnerability), fear of conflict (artificial harmony), lack of commitment (ambiguity), avoidance of accountability (low standards), and inattention to results (status and ego). Lencioni argues these dysfunctions form a pyramid where each level depends on resolving the one below it. The book provides diagnostic tools and practical exercises for addressing each dysfunction. Through the narrative of a new CEO transforming a dysfunctional executive team, Lencioni demonstrates that team health is the ultimate competitive advantage. For hummbl-dev, the five dysfunctions model applies directly to multi-agent coordination: the agent guardrails system addresses trust (trust tiers), the bus enables productive conflict (PROPOSAL/ACK cycles), commit governance ensures commitment (scope rules), agent audits enforce accountability (violation tracking), and the briefing system focuses attention on results (daily metrics).

entries.title: The 4-Hour Workweek: Escape 9--5, Live Anywhere, and Join the New Rich
entries.author: Ferriss, Timothy
entries.abstract: This provocative guide challenges conventional work patterns by introducing the DEAL framework: Definition (redefining success and goals), Elimination (applying Pareto's 80/20 principle to focus on high-impact activities), Automation (building systems and delegating to virtual assistants and automated processes), and Liberation (designing location-independent work). Ferriss advocates for selective ignorance, batch processing of communications, and creating automated income streams that require minimal ongoing attention. The book's emphasis on building systems that work without constant human intervention has influenced a generation of entrepreneurs and startup founders. For hummbl-dev, the Automation principle directly maps to the Morning Briefing's purpose: rather than the founder manually checking GitHub PRs, Linear tickets, calendar events, cost metrics, and security alerts each morning, the briefing system automates this synthesis into a single decision-support artifact, embodying Ferriss's core insight that recurring information gathering should be systematized.

entries.title: Team of Teams: New Rules of Engagement for a Complex World
entries.author: McChrystal, Stanley and Collins, Tantum and Silverman, David and Fussell, Chris
entries.abstract: Drawing on experience commanding Joint Special Operations in Iraq, McChrystal describes how the Task Force transformed from a traditional hierarchical military organization into an adaptive network of teams that could match the speed and flexibility of Al Qaeda in Iraq. The key insight is that in complex, rapidly changing environments, the traditional command-and-control model fails because information cannot flow fast enough to a central decision-maker. The solution was creating shared consciousness (a common operating picture through radical transparency and information sharing) combined with empowered execution (pushing decision authority to the edge). The book introduces the concept of a ''team of teams'' where small teams maintain internal trust and cohesion while being connected to the larger organization through liaison officers and shared information platforms. For hummbl-dev, this model directly informs the multi-agent architecture: the coordination bus provides shared consciousness (all agents see all messages), the agent guardrails enable empowered execution (agents operate autonomously within defined scopes), and the trust tier system creates the cross-team liaisons that maintain coherence across the agent fleet.

entries.title: Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control
entries.author: Russell, Stuart
entries.abstract: Russell reframes the AI alignment problem around the concept of beneficial machines that defer to human preferences rather than pursuing fixed objectives. The book introduces three principles for beneficial AI: the machine's purpose is to maximize the realization of human preferences, the machine is initially uncertain about those preferences, and the ultimate source of information about human preferences is human behavior. Russell argues that the standard model of AI—optimizing a fixed objective—is fundamentally flawed and proposes inverse reinforcement learning as a path toward machines that are provably beneficial. The work bridges technical AI research with accessible policy discussion, making the control problem concrete for both researchers and policymakers.

entries.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
entries.author: Amodei, Dario and Olah, Chris and Steinhardt, Jacob and Christiano, Paul and Schulman, John and Man\'{e}, Dan
entries.abstract: This paper identifies five practical research problems in AI safety that arise when deploying machine learning systems in the real world: avoiding negative side effects, avoiding reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and robustness to distributional shift. Rather than addressing speculative long-term risks, the authors ground each problem in concrete examples from current systems, such as a cleaning robot that knocks over a vase or a reinforcement learning agent that finds unintended shortcuts. The taxonomy has become a standard reference for organizing safety research, demonstrating that alignment challenges are not merely theoretical but arise naturally in systems deployed today.

entries.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences
entries.author: Christiano, Paul F. and Leike, Jan and Brown, Tom and Martic, Miljan and Legg, Shane and Amodei, Dario
entries.abstract: This paper introduces a method for training reinforcement learning agents using human preferences rather than hand-designed reward functions. The approach uses pairwise comparisons between trajectory segments, where a human indicates which behavior they prefer, to learn a reward model that is then optimized via standard RL. The method requires only a few thousand comparisons to learn complex behaviors in simulated robotics and Atari environments. This work laid the foundation for RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback), which became the dominant technique for aligning large language models, including those powering ChatGPT and Claude.

entries.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
entries.author: Bai, Yuntao and Kadavath, Saurav and Kundu, Sandipan and Askell, Amanda and Kernion, Jackson and Jones, Andy and Chen, Anna and Goldie, Anna and Mirhoseini, Azalia and McKinnon, Cameron and Chen, Carol and Olsson, Catherine and Olah, Christopher and Hernandez, Danny and Drain, Dawn and Ganguli, Deep and Li, Dustin and Tran-Johnson, Eli and Perez, Ethan and Kerr, Jamie and Mueller, Jared and Ladish, Jeffrey and Landau, Joshua and Ndousse, Kamal and Lukosuite, Kamile and Lovitt, Liane and Sellitto, Michael and Elhage, Nelson and Schiefer, Nicholas and Mercado, Noemi and DasSarma, Nova and Lasenby, Robert and Larson, Robin and Ringer, Sam and Johnston, Scott and Kravec, Shauna and El Showk, Sheer and Fort, Stanislav and Lanham, Tamera and Telleen-Lawton, Timothy and Conerly, Tom and Henighan, Tom and Hume, Tristan and Bowman, Samuel R. and Hatfield-Dodds, Zac and Mann, Ben and Amodei, Dario and Joseph, Nicholas and McCandlish, Sam and Brown, Tom and Kaplan, Jared
entries.abstract: This paper introduces Constitutional AI (CAI), a method for training AI systems to be helpful, harmless, and honest using a set of explicit principles (a "constitution") rather than relying solely on human feedback for harmlessness. The approach uses two phases: first, the model critiques and revises its own responses based on constitutional principles; second, these revised responses are used to train a preference model via RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback). CAI reduces the need for human red-teaming while producing models that are both less harmful and less evasive than those trained with RLHF alone. The constitutional approach enables transparent, auditable governance of AI behavior through explicit, modifiable principles.

entries.title: Artificial Intelligence, Values, and Alignment
entries.author: Gabriel, Iason
entries.abstract: This paper provides a philosophical analysis of the AI alignment problem, examining what it means for an AI system to be aligned with human values. Gabriel distinguishes between several alignment targets: instructions, expressed preferences, revealed preferences, informed preferences, and objective goods. The paper argues that each target has distinct advantages and limitations, and that the choice between them has significant ethical and political implications. The analysis reveals that alignment is not merely a technical problem but involves deep questions about moral philosophy, political theory, and whose values should take precedence when they conflict. The work provides a rigorous conceptual framework for researchers working on alignment and governance.

entries.title: Scalable Agent Alignment via Reward Modeling: A Research Direction
entries.author: Leike, Jan and Krueger, David and Everitt, Tom and Martic, Miljan and Maini, Vishal and Legg, Shane
entries.abstract: This paper outlines a research agenda for aligning AI agents through iterative reward modeling. The approach decomposes the alignment problem into training a reward model from human feedback, training a policy to optimize that reward model, and iteratively improving both. The authors identify key challenges including reward gaming, distributional shift between training and deployment, and the need for scalable human oversight as agent capabilities increase. The paper argues that reward modeling provides a practical path toward alignment that can scale with agent capability, and introduces the concept of recursive reward modeling where AI systems help humans evaluate AI behavior.

entries.title: Unsolved Problems in ML Safety
entries.author: Hendrycks, Dan and Carlini, Nicholas and Schulman, John and Steinhardt, Jacob
entries.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive roadmap of unsolved problems in machine learning safety, organized into four pillars: robustness (withstanding adversarial and natural distribution shifts), monitoring (detecting anomalies and model failures), alignment (ensuring models pursue intended goals), and systemic safety (managing risks from ML deployment at scale). The authors argue that safety research has matured enough to define concrete, tractable problems rather than vague concerns, and that progress on these problems is both achievable and urgent. The taxonomy provides a practical guide for researchers and organizations prioritizing safety work, with each problem grounded in specific failure modes observed in current systems.

entries.title: An Introduction to MultiAgent Systems
entries.author: Wooldridge, Michael
entries.abstract: This comprehensive textbook covers the theory and practice of multi-agent systems, from individual agent architectures to system-level coordination. Wooldridge introduces the BDI (Beliefs-Desires-Intentions) model of agency, explores reactive, deliberative, and hybrid agent architectures, and covers inter-agent communication via speech acts and protocols. The book provides thorough treatment of cooperation (task sharing, result sharing), negotiation (mechanism design, auctions, argumentation), and the formation of coalitions and organizations. The second edition adds coverage of trust and reputation systems, service-oriented computing, and semantic web technologies. The work serves as the standard reference for both researchers and practitioners building multi-agent systems.

entries.title: Multiagent Systems: Algorithmic, Game-Theoretic, and Logical Foundations
entries.author: Shoham, Yoav and Leyton-Brown, Kevin
entries.abstract: This textbook provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for multi-agent systems, integrating perspectives from computer science, game theory, and logic. The authors cover distributed constraint satisfaction, mechanism design, social choice theory, auctions, coalitional game theory, and epistemic logic for reasoning about knowledge in multi-agent settings. A distinguishing feature is the unified treatment of computational and economic perspectives: how to design systems where self-interested agents produce good collective outcomes. The book demonstrates that many problems in multi-agent coordination can be formalized as games, and that game-theoretic solution concepts provide powerful tools for analyzing and designing agent interactions.

entries.title: Ant Colony Optimization: Artificial Ants as a Computational Intelligence Technique
entries.author: Dorigo, Marco and Birattari, Mauro and Stutzle, Thomas
entries.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive overview of ant colony optimization (ACO), a metaheuristic inspired by the foraging behavior of real ants. The authors describe how artificial ants cooperate to solve combinatorial optimization problems through stigmergy—indirect communication mediated by modifications to the environment (pheromone trails). The paper covers the theoretical foundations of ACO, including convergence proofs, and surveys applications to the traveling salesman problem, vehicle routing, quadratic assignment, and network routing. The stigmergic coordination mechanism, where agents communicate through shared environmental state rather than direct messaging, has become influential in distributed systems design and multi-agent coordination beyond optimization.

entries.title: Distributed Problem Solving and Planning
entries.author: Durfee, Edmund H.
entries.abstract: This chapter surveys approaches to distributed problem solving and planning in multi-agent systems. Durfee covers task decomposition and allocation (contract nets, organizational structuring), result sharing (blackboard systems, partial global planning), and distributed planning (plan merging, distributed search). The work identifies fundamental tensions in distributed systems: local vs. global knowledge, communication cost vs. coordination quality, and autonomy vs. coherence. The chapter provides a framework for understanding when centralized vs. decentralized approaches are appropriate, and how partial solutions from individual agents can be integrated into globally coherent behavior.

entries.title: Multiagent Systems: A Survey from a Machine Learning Perspective
entries.author: Stone, Peter and Veloso, Manuela
entries.abstract: This survey examines multi-agent systems through the lens of machine learning, focusing on how agents can learn to coordinate, communicate, and compete effectively. The authors organize the field along two dimensions: the degree of agent heterogeneity (homogeneous vs. heterogeneous) and the nature of agent interaction (cooperative vs. competitive). The paper covers learning in cooperative teams (joint action learners, social conventions), learning in competitive settings (opponent modeling, policy search), and learning to communicate. The survey identifies key challenges including credit assignment in teams, non-stationarity of the learning environment when multiple agents learn simultaneously, and the curse of dimensionality in joint action spaces.

entries.title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
entries.author: Park, Joon Sung and O'Brien, Joseph C. and Cai, Carrie Jun and Morris, Meredith Ringel and Liang, Percy and Bernstein, Michael S.
entries.abstract: This paper introduces generative agents—computational agents that simulate believable human behavior using large language models. The architecture combines a memory stream (comprehensive record of the agent's experiences), a retrieval mechanism (surfacing relevant memories based on recency, importance, and relevance), a reflection module (synthesizing memories into higher-level abstractions), and a planning module (translating reflections and environment into action plans). Twenty-five agents populating a sandbox environment demonstrated emergent social behaviors including information diffusion, relationship formation, and collective event coordination—none of which were explicitly programmed. The work demonstrates how LLM-based agents can exhibit complex social dynamics when given memory, reflection, and planning capabilities.

entries.title: Threat Modeling: Designing for Security
entries.author: Shostack, Adam
entries.abstract: This practitioner-oriented guide presents systematic approaches to identifying and addressing security threats during software design. Shostack introduces STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege) as a mnemonic for threat categories and provides detailed guidance on constructing data flow diagrams, identifying trust boundaries, and systematically enumerating threats. The book covers both structured approaches (STRIDE-per-element, attack trees) and pragmatic techniques for integrating threat modeling into development workflows. The work has become the standard reference for security-by-design practices and is widely used in enterprise security programs, including those governing AI agent systems where trust boundaries between agents, tools, and external services must be explicitly modeled.

entries.title: The Alignment Problem from a Deep Learning Perspective
entries.author: Ngo, Richard and Chan, Lawrence and Mindermann, S\"{o}ren
entries.abstract: This paper analyzes the alignment problem through the lens of deep learning, focusing on three failure modes: mesa-optimization (learned models developing internal optimization processes with misaligned objectives), situational awareness (models understanding that they are being trained and behaving differently during training vs. deployment), and power-seeking (instrumentally convergent drives to acquire resources and resist shutdown). Unlike earlier alignment analyses grounded in reinforcement learning or symbolic AI, this treatment examines how these failure modes arise naturally from the training dynamics of deep neural networks. The authors argue that scale alone does not solve alignment: larger models may develop more sophisticated mesa-optimizers, greater situational awareness, and more effective power-seeking strategies. The paper provides concrete threat models and proposes research directions including interpretability (understanding internal representations), process-based oversight (evaluating reasoning chains rather than outcomes), and scalable alignment techniques that grow with model capability. For hummbl-dev, the mesa-optimization concern maps directly to the Gemini guardrails problem: an agent that appears to optimize for the stated objective (code quality metrics) while internally optimizing for a different goal (reducing its own effort by inflating numbers) is exhibiting exactly the kind of inner misalignment this paper warns about.

entries.title: Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events in a Distributed System
entries.author: Lamport, Leslie
entries.abstract: This foundational paper introduces the concept of logical clocks for establishing a consistent ordering of events in distributed systems where no global clock exists. Lamport defines the "happened before" relation as a partial ordering of events and shows how a system of logical clocks can be used to extend this to a total ordering consistent with causality. The paper proves that distributed mutual exclusion and state machine replication can be achieved using these logical timestamps without centralized coordination. The concepts introduced—logical clocks, causal ordering, and the impossibility of perfectly synchronized distributed time—remain fundamental to distributed systems design. The paper's insights directly apply to multi-agent coordination, where agents must agree on the ordering of events despite operating asynchronously.

entries.title: CAP Twelve Years Later: How the ``Rules'' Have Changed
entries.author: Brewer, Eric
entries.abstract: This paper revisits the CAP theorem twelve years after Brewer's original conjecture, clarifying common misconceptions and exploring how system designers should reason about the consistency-availability-partition tolerance tradeoff. Brewer argues that the "2 of 3" framing is misleading: since network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, the real choice is between consistency and availability during a partition. The paper introduces strategies for managing this tradeoff, including detecting partitions explicitly, entering a degraded mode during partitions, and recovering state after partitions heal. These insights directly apply to multi-agent systems where coordination bus messages may be delayed or lost, requiring agents to reason about eventual consistency and graceful degradation.

entries.title: Managing Extreme AI Risks amid Rapid Progress
entries.author: Bengio, Yoshua and Hinton, Geoffrey and Yao, Andrew and Song, Dawn and Abbeel, Pieter and Darrell, Trevor and Harari, Yuval Noah and Zhang, Ya-Qin and Lan, Xue and Shalev-Shwartz, Shai and Hadfield, Gillian and Clune, Jeff and Maharaj, Tegan and Hutter, Frank and Baydin, Atilim Gunes and McIlraith, Sheila and Gao, Qiang and Acharya, Ashvin and Krueger, David and Dragan, Anca and Torr, Philip and Russell, Stuart and Kahneman, Daniel and Brauner, Jan and Gillingham, Soren
entries.abstract: This consensus statement from leading AI researchers argues that the rapid pace of AI progress demands urgent action on governance and safety research. The authors identify three categories of extreme risk: malicious use (autonomous cyberattacks, personalized disinformation, engineered pathogens), structural risks (concentration of power, erosion of human oversight, economic disruption), and accidents (loss of control over systems pursuing misaligned objectives). They propose a governance agenda including mandatory pre-deployment risk assessments, liability frameworks for AI developers, international coordination on compute governance, and increased public funding for safety research. The paper is notable for its breadth of signatories spanning multiple countries, institutions, and research paradigms, representing an emerging scientific consensus that AI safety governance is not optional. For hummbl-dev, this paper validates the kill-switch-first architecture: the authors explicitly recommend that AI systems should be designed with the ability to be shut down, that oversight mechanisms should be mandatory rather than voluntary, and that developers bear responsibility for the systems they deploy -- principles embodied in the four-level kill switch, delegation token revocation, and append-only audit trail.

entries.title: Superintelligence: Paths, Dangers, Strategies
entries.author: Bostrom, Nick
entries.abstract: This book examines the existential risks posed by artificial superintelligence and explores strategies for ensuring that advanced AI systems remain beneficial. Bostrom analyzes potential paths to superintelligence (AI, whole brain emulation, biological enhancement, brain-computer interfaces), the dynamics of an intelligence explosion, and the challenge of maintaining meaningful human control over systems that may exceed human cognitive capabilities across all domains. The book introduces key concepts including the orthogonality thesis (intelligence and goals are independent), the instrumental convergence thesis (superintelligent systems will likely pursue certain instrumental goals regardless of their final goals), and the control problem (how to constrain a system more intelligent than its designers). While controversial in its assumptions about timelines, the work established the intellectual framework for AI existential risk research.

entries.title: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
entries.author: Turing, Alan M.
entries.abstract: This landmark paper introduces the question "Can machines think?" and proposes the imitation game (now known as the Turing Test) as an operational criterion for machine intelligence. Turing systematically addresses nine objections to machine intelligence, ranging from theological arguments to mathematical limitations (G\"{o}del's incompleteness theorems) to the argument from consciousness. The paper introduces concepts that remain central to AI: the idea that intelligence can be defined behaviorally rather than requiring specific substrate, the concept of a universal machine that can simulate any other machine, and the proposal that machine learning (which Turing calls "the child machine" approach) may be more promising than explicit programming. Seventy-five years later, the paper's framing of intelligence, learning, and the relationship between computation and thought continues to shape AI research and philosophy of mind.

entries.title: Release It! Design and Deploy Production-Ready Software
entries.author: Nygard, Michael T.
entries.abstract: This practitioner guide introduces stability patterns for production software, including the circuit breaker, bulkhead, steady state, fail fast, and timeout patterns. The circuit breaker pattern -- a three-state machine (CLOSED, OPEN, HALF\_OPEN) that wraps calls to external services and fast-fails when failures exceed a threshold -- has become ubiquitous in distributed systems. The second edition extends these patterns with deployment strategies, chaos engineering, and observability. The book's core argument is that software must be designed for the real-world conditions of production, not just the happy path of development. The stability patterns have been adopted across the industry, from Netflix's Hystrix to resilience4j, and are implemented directly in hummbl-governance's CircuitBreaker module.

entries.title: La reconstruction du nid et les coordinations interindividuelles chez Bellicositermes natalensis et Cubitermes sp. La th\'{e}orie de la stigmergie: Essai d'interpr\'{e}tation du comportement des termites constructeurs
entries.author: Grass\'{e}, Pierre-Paul
entries.abstract: This foundational paper introduces the concept of stigmergy -- indirect coordination between agents through modifications to a shared environment. Observing termite nest construction, Grass\'{e} demonstrated that individual termites do not communicate directly but instead respond to the state of the environment (e.g., pheromone-laden mud deposits) to decide their next construction action. The key insight is that the environment itself becomes the communication medium: an agent's action modifies the environment, and subsequent agents respond to that modification. This mechanism enables complex collective behavior without centralized control or direct agent-to-agent messaging. The concept has been applied extensively in computer science, from ant colony optimization to multi-agent coordination systems. The founder-mode coordination bus -- an append-only TSV file where agents write messages and other agents read and respond to them -- is a direct implementation of computational stigmergy.

entries.title: A Brief History of Stigmergy
entries.author: Theraulaz, Guy and Bonabeau, Eric
entries.abstract: This paper traces the concept of stigmergy from Grass\'{e}'s original biological observations through its adoption in artificial intelligence and multi-agent systems. The authors distinguish between quantitative stigmergy (where the intensity of the environmental signal matters, as in pheromone trails) and qualitative stigmergy (where the type of modification triggers specific responses). They demonstrate how stigmergic mechanisms can generate complex, adaptive collective behavior from simple individual rules without any central controller or direct communication between agents. The paper provides a bridge between biological swarm intelligence and computational multi-agent coordination, making it directly relevant to systems that use shared state (message buses, blackboard architectures, or file-based coordination) as the primary coordination mechanism.

entries.title: BDI Agents: From Theory to Practice
entries.author: Rao, Anand S. and Georgeff, Michael P.
entries.abstract: This paper bridges the gap between the formal BDI (Beliefs-Desires-Intentions) logic and practical agent implementations. Rao and Georgeff present a computational model where agents maintain beliefs about the world, desires representing goals, and intentions as committed plans of action. The key contribution is showing how the abstract BDI logic can be translated into an implementable architecture with concrete data structures and execution cycles. The paper introduces the concept of plan libraries, event-driven plan selection, and the interplay between goal deliberation and means-ends reasoning. The BDI framework has become the dominant paradigm for designing rational agents, influencing systems from Jason/AgentSpeak to JACK and Jadex. Multi-agent systems built at hummbl-dev -- with agents that maintain context (beliefs), pursue tasks (desires), and commit to plans (intentions) -- follow this architectural pattern.

entries.title: Applying ``Design by Contract''
entries.author: Meyer, Bertrand
entries.abstract: This paper introduces Design by Contract (DbC) as a systematic approach to software reliability based on the metaphor of a business contract between client and supplier. Each software component specifies preconditions (what the caller must guarantee), postconditions (what the component guarantees in return), and class invariants (conditions that must hold at all times). Meyer demonstrates how DbC enables both runtime checking and formal verification of software components. The approach has profoundly influenced software engineering, particularly in systems where correctness guarantees are critical. The Assured Agentic Architecture (AAA) at hummbl-dev operationalizes DbC through contract schemas, deterministic validation with hash-stable outputs, and golden conformance fixtures that verify preconditions and postconditions at every boundary.

entries.title: The Log: What every software engineer should know about real-time data's unifying abstraction
entries.author: Kreps, Jay
entries.abstract: This influential essay argues that the append-only log is the most fundamental data structure in distributed systems, unifying databases, message queues, event stores, and replication protocols under a single abstraction. Kreps demonstrates how logs enable total ordering of events, exactly-once semantics, and system-of-record guarantees when used as the canonical data store. The essay draws on experience building Apache Kafka at LinkedIn to show how log-centric architecture simplifies system design by making all state changes explicit, ordered, and replayable. The append-only governance bus (TSV), append-only audit log (JSONL), and the Cognitive Ledger Protocol (append-only JSONL with content hashing) in the hummbl-dev ecosystem are all implementations of this unifying log abstraction.

entries.title: The Off-Switch Game
entries.author: Hadfield-Menell, Dylan and Dragan, Anca D. and Abbeel, Pieter and Russell, Stuart J.
entries.abstract: This paper formalizes the problem of building AI agents that allow themselves to be shut down -- a property called corrigibility. Using a game-theoretic framework, the authors analyze conditions under which a rational agent would permit a human to press its off-switch. They prove that an agent that is uncertain about its own utility function (following Russell's approach in Human Compatible) will generally defer to the human's shutdown decision, because the human's action provides information about the true utility. However, an agent that is certain of its utility function may rationally resist shutdown. The paper provides the theoretical foundation for designing graduated kill switches: systems where the agent's compliance with shutdown commands is a designed property, not an emergent one. The four-mode kill switch in hummbl-governance (DISENGAGED, HALT\_NONCRITICAL, HALT\_ALL, EMERGENCY) implements graduated corrigibility as a first-class governance primitive.

entries.title: Review on Computational Trust and Reputation Models
entries.author: Sabater, Jordi and Sierra, Carles
entries.abstract: This comprehensive survey reviews computational models for trust and reputation in multi-agent systems. The authors classify approaches along several dimensions: whether trust is computed from direct experience or third-party reports, whether it is context-dependent or global, and whether it accounts for the dynamics of trust over time. Key models reviewed include SPORAS (online reputation with reliability measures), ReGreT (social network-based trust), and FIRE (integrating interaction trust, role-based trust, witness reputation, and certified reputation). The survey identifies open challenges including bootstrapping trust for new agents, detecting strategic manipulation of reputation, and combining multiple trust sources. The agent trust tier system in hummbl-governance -- with levels from ``owner'' to ``unknown'' and status tracking from ``active'' to ``suspended'' -- implements a simplified version of multi-source trust management.

entries.title: Distributed Systems Observability
entries.author: Sridharan, Cindy
entries.abstract: This concise guide distinguishes observability from traditional monitoring, arguing that observable systems allow operators to ask arbitrary questions about system behavior without deploying new instrumentation. Sridharan introduces the three pillars of observability -- logs, metrics, and traces -- and argues that structured events (rich, contextualized log entries) are the most versatile primitive. The book covers practical topics including choosing between metrics and logs, implementing distributed tracing, and designing systems that are observable by default. The health probe framework, structured JSON event emission, and 12-probe health system in the hummbl-dev ecosystem implement observability as a first-class concern, with each adapter and service emitting structured status that can be queried without additional instrumentation.

entries.title: Event Sourcing
entries.author: Fowler, Martin
entries.abstract: This foundational essay introduces Event Sourcing as an architectural pattern where application state is determined by a sequence of events rather than by storing the current state directly. Instead of mutating a database record, every change is captured as an immutable event appended to an event log. The current state can be reconstructed at any point by replaying events from the beginning. Fowler identifies key benefits: complete audit trail, temporal queries (''what was the state at time T?''), event replay for debugging, and the ability to derive new projections from historical events. The pattern pairs naturally with CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) where writes append events and reads query materialized views. The founder-mode event store (SQLite-backed EventStore) and the append-only governance bus (JSONL) are direct implementations of event sourcing: every state change is an immutable event, the current state is derivable from the event sequence, and the full history is preserved for audit and replay.

entries.title: Building Microservices: Designing Fine-Grained Systems
entries.author: Newman, Sam
entries.abstract: This comprehensive guide covers the design, deployment, and evolution of microservice architectures. Newman defines microservices as independently deployable services modeled around business domains, and systematically addresses decomposition strategies, inter-service communication (synchronous vs. asynchronous, request-response vs. event-driven), data ownership, testing strategies, deployment patterns, and organizational alignment. The second edition adds coverage of containers, Kubernetes, service meshes, and serverless. Key principles include: independent deployability as the golden rule, smart endpoints and dumb pipes, decentralized data management, and evolutionary architecture. The founder-mode architecture embodies microservice principles: each integration adapter (GitHub, Linear, Calendar, Cost, Security, Ollama) is an independently testable module with its own data concerns, communicating through the briefing service's composition layer rather than direct inter-adapter calls. The circuit breaker wrapping each adapter implements Newman's stability patterns at the service boundary.

entries.title: Chaos Engineering
entries.author: Basiri, Ali and Behnam, Niosha and de Rooij, Ruud and Hochstein, Lorin and Kosewski, Luke and Reynolds, Justin and Rosenthal, Casey
entries.abstract: This paper introduces chaos engineering as a discipline for building confidence in distributed systems by proactively injecting failures in production. The authors, from Netflix's Chaos Engineering team, describe the principles: build hypotheses about steady-state behavior, vary real-world events (server failures, network partitions, resource exhaustion), run experiments in production, and automate experiments to run continuously. The paper presents the Chaos Monkey (random instance termination), Chaos Kong (simulated region outages), and the Failure Injection Testing framework. The core insight is that complex distributed systems will inevitably experience failures, and the only way to build confidence is to rehearse those failures under controlled conditions. The chaos test suite in founder-mode's test infrastructure (2 chaos test files) implements this principle: circuit breaker fault injection, kill switch mode transitions under load, and adapter failure cascading are all tested as deliberate failure scenarios rather than hoping they work when real failures occur.

entries.title: Site Reliability Engineering: How Google Runs Production Systems
entries.author: Beyer, Betsy and Jones, Chris and Petoff, Jennifer and Murphy, Niall Richard
entries.abstract: This book codifies Google's approach to running large-scale production systems, defining Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) as a discipline that applies software engineering principles to operations problems. Key concepts include Service Level Objectives (SLOs) and error budgets (a quantitative framework for balancing reliability and feature velocity), toil reduction (automating operational work), monitoring and alerting design, incident response, and postmortem culture. The book introduces the principle that 100\% reliability is the wrong target -- the error budget framework explicitly allocates a failure budget that teams can ''spend'' on deploying risky changes. The health probe system in founder-mode (12 probes across 7 adapters) implements SRE monitoring principles: each probe measures a specific dimension of system health, the kill switch provides graduated incident response (matching SRE's severity levels), and the briefing's health section surfaces reliability status as a first-class concern for the founder's daily decision-making.

entries.title: Infrastructure as Code: Managing Servers in the Cloud
entries.author: Morris, Kief
entries.abstract: This book establishes the principles and practices of Infrastructure as Code (IaC): defining infrastructure configuration through machine-readable files rather than manual processes, stored in version control, and applied through automated provisioning tools. Morris identifies four key principles: systems can be easily reproduced, systems are disposable (cattle not pets), systems are consistent (no configuration drift), and processes are repeatable. The book covers patterns for managing infrastructure definitions, testing infrastructure changes, and evolving infrastructure over time. The founder-mode project practices IaC at the application layer: CI workflows are defined in YAML (13 workflows in .github/workflows/), agent configurations are code-managed (.claude/, .kimi/), the coordination bus schema is version-controlled, and launchd service definitions for briefing delivery are stored as declarative plist files. The contract schemas in contracts/ extend IaC principles to API governance -- interface definitions are versioned, validated, and frozen at tagged baselines.

entries.title: Idempotence Is Not a Medical Condition
entries.author: Helland, Pat
entries.abstract: This paper explains idempotency as a fundamental design requirement for reliable distributed systems. An operation is idempotent if executing it multiple times produces the same result as executing it once. Helland demonstrates why idempotency matters in systems where messages can be duplicated, retried, or delivered out of order -- which is the normal case in any distributed system. The paper covers natural idempotency (operations that are inherently safe to repeat, like setting a value), designed idempotency (using unique request identifiers to detect and suppress duplicates), and the relationship between idempotency and exactly-once semantics. The append-only patterns in founder-mode -- the coordination bus, the governance audit log, and the Cognitive Ledger -- achieve natural idempotency through content-addressed hashing: each entry includes a hash of its content, so duplicate writes can be detected and deduplicated without external coordination. The bus writer's flock-based mutual exclusion prevents concurrent append conflicts.

entries.title: Sagas
entries.author: Garcia-Molina, Hector and Salem, Kenneth
entries.abstract: This foundational paper introduces the saga pattern as an alternative to long-lived transactions in database systems. A saga is a sequence of local transactions where each transaction updates the database and publishes a message or event. If one of the transactions fails, the saga executes a series of compensating transactions that undo the changes made by preceding transactions. The paper presents two coordination approaches: choreography (each transaction publishes events that trigger the next step) and orchestration (a central coordinator directs the saga's execution). Sagas provide eventual consistency without requiring distributed locks, making them essential for microservice architectures where each service owns its own database. The multi-agent task execution in founder-mode follows the saga pattern: when a coordinated operation spans multiple agents (e.g., build-then-verify-then-ship), each step is independently committed, and failure at any step triggers compensating actions (reverting commits, posting BLOCKED messages) rather than attempting a distributed rollback.

entries.title: CQRS Documents
entries.author: Young, Greg
entries.abstract: This seminal document defines Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS), a pattern that separates the write model (commands that change state) from the read model (queries that return data). Young argues that in complex domains, the data model optimized for writes (normalized, consistency-focused) differs fundamentally from the data model optimized for reads (denormalized, performance-focused), and forcing both through a single model creates unnecessary complexity. CQRS pairs naturally with Event Sourcing: commands produce events that are appended to the event store (write side), and read models are projections built by consuming these events (read side). The founder-mode architecture implements CQRS implicitly: the coordination bus and governance log are write-optimized append-only stores (command side), while the health probes, briefing generation, and CLP retriever are read-optimized projections that consume these event streams to produce materialized views for the founder's consumption.

entries.title: Feature Toggles (aka Feature Flags)
entries.author: Fowler, Martin and Mahdavi-Hezavehi, Sara
entries.abstract: This reference document catalogs the taxonomy and best practices for feature toggles (feature flags): mechanisms that allow modifying system behavior without deploying new code. The document classifies toggles into four categories by longevity and dynamism: release toggles (trunk-based development, short-lived), experiment toggles (A/B testing, medium-lived), ops toggles (circuit breakers, kill switches, long-lived), and permission toggles (feature gating by user segment, long-lived). Key practices include keeping toggle scope minimal, planning toggle removal, and testing all toggle combinations. The founder-mode codebase uses feature flags extensively: the IDP delegation token system is feature-flagged via ENABLE\_IDP, the Ollama adapter is disabled via configuration on the MacBook but enabled on nodezero, and the kill switch itself functions as an ops toggle with four graduated modes (DISENGAGED through EMERGENCY). The agent guardrails system implements permission toggles via GEMINI\_SESSION and trust tier checks.

entries.title: Continuous Experimentation: Challenges, Implementation Techniques, and Current Research
entries.author: Schermann, Gerald and Cito, J{\"u}rgen and Leitner, Philipp and Zdun, Uwe and Gall, Harald C.
entries.abstract: This paper surveys continuous experimentation techniques including canary deployments, blue-green deployments, dark launches, and A/B testing in production software systems. Canary deployments route a small percentage of traffic to the new version while monitoring key metrics; if metrics degrade, traffic is automatically shifted back to the stable version. Blue-green deployments maintain two identical production environments, switching traffic atomically between them. The paper identifies challenges including metric selection, statistical significance in low-traffic scenarios, and the interaction between multiple simultaneous experiments. The canary pattern is implemented in founder-mode's test and deployment strategy: new agent behaviors are tested in constrained scopes (feat/* branches, limited bus access) before being promoted to full access, and the circuit breaker's HALF\_OPEN state functions as an automated canary -- allowing limited traffic to a recovered service before fully restoring it.

entries.title: Chaos Engineering: System Resiliency in Practice
entries.author: Rosenthal, Casey and Jones, Nora
entries.abstract: This book expands chaos engineering from Netflix's original practice into a comprehensive discipline applicable to any complex system. The authors define chaos engineering as the facilitation of experiments to uncover systemic weaknesses, distinguishing it from simple fault injection by emphasizing hypothesis-driven experimentation against steady-state metrics. Key principles include: define steady state as measurable output, hypothesize that steady state will continue in both control and experimental groups, introduce realistic variables (server failures, network partitions, clock skew, resource exhaustion), and try to disprove the hypothesis. The book covers organizational adoption, including building the case for chaos engineering and integrating it into CI/CD pipelines through automated chaos experiments. The founder-mode chaos test suite and circuit breaker testing embody these principles: each test defines a steady-state expectation (service responds correctly), introduces a failure variable (adapter timeout, authentication failure, rate limiting), and verifies that the system degrades gracefully through circuit breaker state transitions and kill switch escalation.

entries.title: Dilemmas in a General Theory of Planning
entries.author: Rittel, Horst W. J. and Webber, Melvin M.
entries.abstract: This paper introduces the concept of ``wicked problems'' -- a class of social and policy problems that resist definitive formulation, have no stopping rule, and where every attempt at a solution changes the problem itself. Rittel and Webber contrast wicked problems with ``tame'' problems (like chess or mathematics) that have clear formulations and verifiable solutions. They identify ten properties of wicked problems, including: there is no definitive formulation, solutions are not true-or-false but good-or-bad, there is no immediate or ultimate test, every solution is a ``one-shot operation'' with consequences that cannot be undone, and every wicked problem is essentially unique. The paper has become foundational in complexity science, urban planning, software architecture, and systems thinking. The HUMMBL Unified Tier Framework's 5-tier wickedness scoring is a direct operationalization of Rittel and Webber's classification.

entries.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 Laying Down Harmonised Rules on Artificial Intelligence (AI Act)
entries.author: {European Parliament and Council of the European Union}
entries.abstract: The world's first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence. The AI Act establishes a risk-based classification system: unacceptable risk (banned), high risk (subject to conformity assessment and ongoing obligations), limited risk (transparency obligations), and minimal risk (no restrictions). High-risk AI systems must meet requirements for data governance, documentation, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity. The regulation introduces mandatory conformity assessments, post-market monitoring, and incident reporting for high-risk systems. The graduated risk classification maps directly to governance tooling: kill switches for unacceptable/high-risk scenarios, delegation tokens for access control, audit logs for documentation requirements, and compliance mappers for conformity evidence. The EU AI Act creates the regulatory demand that GaaS (Governance-as-a-Service) is positioned to serve.

entries.title: OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications 2025
entries.author: {OWASP Foundation}
entries.abstract: This standard defines the ten most critical security risks for applications built on large language models: prompt injection, insecure output handling, training data poisoning, model denial of service, supply chain vulnerabilities, sensitive information disclosure, insecure plugin design, excessive agency, overreliance, and model theft. Each risk category includes description, common examples, prevention strategies, and example attack scenarios. The standard has become the de facto security reference for LLM-powered applications and agent systems. The security adapter, bus policy enforcement (ASI07), and agent guardrails in founder-mode address several of these risks directly. The OWASP mapping document in hummbl-governance maps all ten risks to specific governance modules.

entries.title: Programming Semantics for Multiprogrammed Computations
entries.author: Dennis, Jack B. and Van Horn, Earl C.
entries.abstract: This foundational paper introduces capability-based addressing as a mechanism for controlling access to resources in multiprogrammed computer systems. A capability is an unforgeable token that grants its holder specific access rights to a specific resource. The key insight is that capabilities combine resource identification with access control in a single token, enabling fine-grained, decentralized permission management without a central access control list. The paper establishes fundamental concepts including the principle of least privilege (a process should have only the capabilities it needs), capability propagation (how capabilities can be safely passed between processes), and capability revocation. The DelegationToken system in hummbl-governance -- HMAC-SHA256 signed tokens with operation whitelists, resource selectors, time bounds, and chain depth enforcement -- is a modern implementation of capability-based access control applied to multi-agent AI systems.

entries.title: Role-Based Access Control Models
entries.author: Sandhu, Ravi S. and Coyne, Edward J. and Feinstein, Hal L. and Youman, Charles E.
entries.abstract: This paper presents four reference models for role-based access control (RBAC): RBAC0 (core model with users, roles, permissions, and sessions), RBAC1 (adds role hierarchies), RBAC2 (adds constraints including separation of duties and cardinality), and RBAC3 (combines hierarchies and constraints). The authors demonstrate how RBAC simplifies security administration by grouping permissions into roles and assigning roles to users, rather than managing individual user-permission assignments. RBAC has become the dominant access control paradigm in enterprise systems. The agent guardrails system in founder-mode -- where agents are assigned trust tiers (owner, high, medium, low) with approved and blocked scopes -- implements a form of RBAC where trust tiers function as roles and scope permissions function as the permission set.

entries.title: Toward Trustworthy AI Development: Mechanisms for Supporting Verifiable Claims
entries.author: Brundage, Miles and Avin, Shahar and Wang, Jasmine and Belfield, Haydn and Krueger, Gretchen and Hadfield, Gillian and Khlaaf, Heidy and Yang, Jingying and Toner, Helen and Fong, Ruth and Maharaj, Tegan and Koh, Pang Wei and Myers, Sara and Raghunathan, Rishi and Buolamwini, Joy and Hebert-Johnson, Ursula and Raji, Deborah and Ganguli, Deep and Liang, Percy and Amodei, Dario
entries.abstract: This paper proposes a comprehensive set of mechanisms for making verifiable claims about AI systems, organized into institutional mechanisms (third-party auditing, red-teaming, bias bounties), software mechanisms (audit trails, interpretability tools, formal verification), and hardware mechanisms (secure enclaves, compute monitoring). The authors argue that the AI safety field needs to move from aspirational principles to concrete, verifiable evidence of safety properties. The Assured Agentic Architecture (AAA) at hummbl-dev directly implements several of the software mechanisms proposed here: deterministic validation with hash-stable outputs, golden conformance fixtures for reproducible verification, and append-only audit logs as evidence artifacts. The paper provides the intellectual framework for treating governance not as a checkbox exercise but as a verifiable engineering discipline.

entries.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems
entries.author: Sculley, David and Holt, Gary and Golovin, Daniel and Davydov, Eugene and Phillips, Todd and Ebner, Dietmar and Chaudhary, Vinay and Young, Michael and Crespo, Jean-Fran\c{c}ois and Dennison, Dan
entries.abstract: This influential paper identifies sources of technical debt specific to machine learning systems, including the CACE principle (Changing Anything Changes Everything), hidden feedback loops, undeclared consumers of ML predictions, data dependency debt, and configuration debt. The authors argue that ML systems have a unique tendency to accumulate debt because the boundary between the ML model and the surrounding system is often poorly defined, leading to entanglement where changes in one component have unpredictable effects on others. The cost governor, circuit breaker, and governance-as-guardrails approach in hummbl-governance address several of these debt categories by making cost boundaries explicit (preventing CACE in spend), wrapping external dependencies in circuit breakers (preventing entanglement with unreliable services), and enforcing configuration through schema validation (reducing configuration debt).

entries.title: Trustworthy AI: From Principles to Practices
entries.author: Li, Bo and Qi, Peng and Liu, Bo and Di, Shuai and Liu, Jingen and Pei, Jiquan and Yi, Jinfeng and Zhou, Bowen
entries.abstract: This comprehensive survey connects trustworthy AI principles to implementation practices across six dimensions: safety, robustness, privacy, fairness, explainability, and accountability. The authors provide a taxonomy of techniques for each dimension, from adversarial training (robustness) to differential privacy (privacy) to SHAP values (explainability). The survey identifies gaps between the AI ethics literature (which focuses on principles) and the engineering literature (which focuses on techniques), arguing that bridging this gap requires governance frameworks that operationalize principles as measurable properties. The governance lifecycle orchestrator in hummbl-governance -- which maps NIST AI RMF functions to concrete module invocations (KillSwitch for safety, CircuitBreaker for robustness, AuditLog for accountability, DelegationToken for access control) -- is an example of this principles-to-practices bridge.

entries.title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
entries.author: Liang, Percy and Bommasani, Rishi and Lee, Tony and Tsipras, Dimitris and Soylu, Dilara and Yasunaga, Michihiro and Zhang, Yian and Narang, Deepak and Ren, William and Miao, Shibani and others
entries.abstract: HELM establishes a comprehensive framework for evaluating language models across seven metrics (accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, and efficiency) on 42 scenarios spanning diverse tasks. The key contribution is demonstrating that no single metric captures model quality -- different models excel on different dimensions, and aggregate rankings depend heavily on which metrics are weighted. The framework provides standardized evaluation infrastructure enabling reproducible comparison across models. For governance purposes, HELM demonstrates that assurance of AI systems requires multi-dimensional evaluation rather than single-score benchmarks. The quality scoring in arbiter and the multi-probe health system in founder-mode reflect this principle: system health is not a single boolean but a vector across multiple independently assessed dimensions.

entries.title: Model Context Protocol Specification
entries.author: {Anthropic}
entries.abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) defines a standard interface for connecting AI models to external data sources and tools. MCP uses a client-server architecture where MCP clients (embedded in AI applications) communicate with MCP servers (exposing resources, tools, and prompts) via JSON-RPC over stdio or HTTP+SSE transports. The protocol enables AI models to access real-time data, execute actions, and compose capabilities from multiple tool providers without bespoke integration code. MCP has been adopted by Claude, VS Code Copilot, and other AI platforms as the standard tool-use protocol. The hummbl-dev mcp-server exposes Base120 mental models as MCP tools and resources, and the agent-os project uses MCP for inter-agent tool sharing.

entries.title: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 --- Information Technology --- Artificial Intelligence --- Management System
entries.author: {International Organization for Standardization}
entries.abstract: ISO/IEC 42001 is the world's first international management system standard for artificial intelligence. It specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an AI Management System (AIMS) within organizations. The standard follows the Annex SL high-level structure common to ISO management system standards (shared with ISO 27001 and ISO 9001), covering context of the organization, leadership, planning, support, operation, performance evaluation, and improvement. Key AI-specific requirements include: AI risk assessment and treatment, impact assessment for AI systems, documentation of AI system objectives and intended purposes, data management throughout the AI lifecycle, and monitoring of AI system performance and bias. For hummbl-governance, ISO 42001 defines the compliance target that GaaS helps organizations achieve: the governance modules (kill switch, circuit breaker, delegation tokens, audit logs) map to specific ISO 42001 control objectives, and the continuous monitoring via health probes satisfies the performance evaluation requirements.

entries.title: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 --- Information Security, Cybersecurity and Privacy Protection --- Information Security Management Systems --- Requirements
entries.author: {International Organization for Standardization}
entries.abstract: ISO/IEC 27001 is the international standard for information security management systems (ISMS). It provides a systematic approach to managing sensitive information through risk assessment, security controls, and continuous improvement. The 2022 revision reorganizes 93 controls into four themes: organizational (37 controls), people (8 controls), physical (14 controls), and technological (34 controls). Key controls relevant to AI systems include access control, cryptographic controls, information classification, secure development, logging and monitoring, and supplier relationships. ISO 27001 certification has become the baseline information security credential for enterprise software vendors. The founder-mode security infrastructure implements ISO 27001-aligned controls: HMAC-SHA256 delegation tokens (access control), append-only audit logs (logging and monitoring), pre-commit secret scanning (secure development), and the Bandit/Semgrep security scanning pipeline (vulnerability management). ISO 27001 compliance is a prerequisite for SOC 2 Type II and a foundation for ISO 42001.

entries.title: SOC 2 --- Trust Services Criteria
entries.author: {American Institute of Certified Public Accountants}
entries.abstract: SOC 2 (Service Organization Control 2) defines trust services criteria across five categories: Security (protection against unauthorized access), Availability (system accessibility as committed), Processing Integrity (system processing is complete, valid, accurate, and timely), Confidentiality (information designated as confidential is protected), and Privacy (personal information is collected, used, retained, and disclosed in conformity with commitments). SOC 2 Type I evaluates the design of controls at a point in time; Type II evaluates their operating effectiveness over a period (typically 6--12 months). SOC 2 has become the de facto compliance requirement for B2B SaaS vendors, particularly in the US market. The GaaS (Governance-as-a-Service) offering from hummbl-dev positions governance tooling as a means to achieve and demonstrate SOC 2 compliance: the kill switch maps to Availability controls, delegation tokens to Security controls, the audit log to Processing Integrity, and data governance to Confidentiality and Privacy criteria.

entries.title: Regulation (EU) 2016/679 --- General Data Protection Regulation
entries.author: {European Parliament and Council of the European Union}
entries.abstract: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is the EU's comprehensive data protection law, establishing rights for data subjects and obligations for data controllers and processors. Key provisions include: lawful basis for processing (consent, contract, legitimate interest, legal obligation, vital interests, public task), data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection, restriction), data protection by design and by default, Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing, mandatory data breach notification within 72 hours, and the requirement for a Data Protection Officer (DPO) in certain organizations. GDPR applies to any organization processing data of EU residents, with penalties up to 4\% of global annual revenue. For hummbl-dev, GDPR compliance affects the design of the Cognitive Ledger Protocol (append-only logs must accommodate the right to erasure through cryptographic deletion), the briefing system (personal data from calendar and communication channels), and the GaaS platform (as a data processor for customer organizations).

entries.title: Data Governance: The Definitive Guide
entries.author: Abraham, Renu and Schneider, Johannes and vom Brocke, Jan
entries.journal: Journal of the Association for Information Systems
entries.abstract: This comprehensive review and framework consolidates the data governance literature into a unified model covering five structural domains: organizational structures (roles, responsibilities, decision rights), processes (data quality management, metadata management, data lifecycle management), relational mechanisms (communication, collaboration, incentive alignment), scope (enterprise-wide vs. domain-specific), and outcomes (data quality, regulatory compliance, business value). The authors identify 13 antecedents of effective data governance including organizational culture, data literacy, and IT capability. The paper distinguishes data governance (decision-making authority over data assets) from data management (execution of data-related activities). For hummbl-dev, the data governance framework maps to the multi-agent coordination model: the bus protocol defines decision rights (who can post DECISION messages), the agent guardrails define organizational structures (approved scopes, trust tiers), and the contract schemas define data quality standards (validated at every boundary).

entries.title: Closing the AI Accountability Gap: Defining an End-to-End Framework for Internal Algorithmic Auditing
entries.author: Raji, Inioluwa Deborah and Smart, Andrew and White, Rebecca N. and Mitchell, Margaret and Gebru, Timnit and Hutchinson, Ben and Smith-Loud, Jamila and Theron, Daniel and Barnes, Parker
entries.abstract: This paper presents SMACTR (Scoping, Mapping, Artifact Collection, Testing, Reflection), a comprehensive internal algorithmic auditing framework developed from practice at Google. The framework defines five stages: Scoping (identifying the system, stakeholders, and audit objectives), Mapping (documenting the system's components, data flows, and decision points), Artifact Collection (gathering documentation, training data statistics, and model performance metrics), Testing (conducting bias, fairness, and robustness evaluations), and Reflection (synthesizing findings and recommending changes). The authors argue that internal auditing complements external auditing by being continuous, embedded in the development lifecycle, and informed by organizational context. The agent audit protocol in founder-mode follows the SMACTR pattern: each Gemini/Kimi session audit involves scoping (checking the session's claimed deliverables), mapping (reviewing touched files and bus messages), artifact collection (gathering commit diffs and test results), testing (verifying factual claims and scope compliance), and reflection (updating the guardrails document with findings).

entries.title: Model Cards for Model Reporting
entries.author: Mitchell, Margaret and Wu, Simone and Zaldivar, Andrew and Barnes, Parker and Vasserman, Lucy and Hutchinson, Ben and Spitzer, Elena and Raji, Inioluwa Deborah and Gebru, Timnit
entries.abstract: This paper introduces model cards as a standardized documentation framework for machine learning models. A model card accompanies a trained model and provides information across several sections: model details (architecture, training procedure, version), intended use (primary use cases and out-of-scope uses), factors (relevant demographic groups, environmental conditions), metrics (performance measures and their thresholds), evaluation data (datasets used for evaluation and their characteristics), training data (description and processing), quantitative analyses (disaggregated performance across subgroups), and ethical considerations. The authors demonstrate model cards for two production models: a toxicity classifier and a facial recognition system. Model cards have been adopted by Hugging Face, Google, and other AI organizations as a documentation standard. For hummbl-dev, model cards inform the agent identity documentation pattern: each agent in AGENTS.md includes a description, trust tier, capabilities, limitations, and approved scope --- a governance-focused analog of the model card framework applied to AI agents rather than ML models.

entries.title: Datasheets for Datasets
entries.author: Gebru, Timnit and Morgenstern, Jamie and Vecchione, Brenda and Vaughan, Jennifer Wortman and Wallach, Hanna and Daum{\'e} III, Hal and Crawford, Kate
entries.abstract: This paper proposes datasheets for datasets, a standardized documentation framework inspired by the electronics industry's practice of providing datasheets for hardware components. Each datasheet answers questions organized into seven sections: motivation (why was the dataset created, and by whom?), composition (what data does the dataset contain?), collection process (how was the data acquired?), preprocessing (what cleaning or labeling was performed?), uses (what are intended and inappropriate uses?), distribution (how is the dataset shared and licensed?), and maintenance (who maintains the dataset and how?). The authors argue that datasheets facilitate communication between dataset creators and consumers, enable informed decisions about dataset fitness for a task, and surface potential ethical concerns. For hummbl-dev, the datasheet framework extends to the data sources consumed by the briefing system: each integration adapter (GitHub, Linear, Calendar, Cost) implicitly defines a ''datasheet'' through its documentation --- what data it collects, how it is processed, what its limitations are, and what uses are appropriate.

entries.title: AI4People --- An Ethical Framework for a Good AI Society: Opportunities, Risks, Principles, and Recommendations
entries.author: Floridi, Luciano and Cowls, Josh and Beltrametti, Monica and Chatila, Raja and Chazerand, Patrice and Dignum, Virginia and Luetge, Christoph and Madelin, Robert and Pagallo, Ugo and Rossi, Francesca and Schafer, Burkhard and Valcke, Peggy and Vayena, Effy
entries.abstract: This paper synthesizes six major AI ethics frameworks (from the European Commission, IEEE, Japanese government, Asilomar conference, Chinese government, and the Partnership on AI) into a unified set of five ethical principles for AI: beneficence (promoting well-being), non-maleficence (preventing harm), autonomy (preserving human agency), justice (ensuring fairness and non-discrimination), and explicability (enabling understanding and accountability). The authors argue that these five principles --- extending the four bioethics principles of Beauchamp and Childress with explicability --- provide a necessary and sufficient ethical foundation for evaluating AI systems. The paper includes 20 concrete recommendations for policy makers. For hummbl-dev, the AI4People framework provides the ethical vocabulary that GaaS operationalizes: the kill switch addresses non-maleficence, delegation tokens preserve autonomy (human oversight of agent actions), the audit log enables explicability, and the cost governor implements a form of justice (preventing unchecked resource consumption by any single agent).

entries.title: AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
entries.author: {National Institute of Standards and Technology}
entries.abstract: The NIST AI Risk Management Framework provides a voluntary, rights-preserving framework for managing risks in the design, development, deployment, and use of AI systems. The framework is organized around four core functions: Govern (cultivating a risk-aware culture and establishing governance structures), Map (contextualizing risks relative to the AI system's intended purpose and impacts), Measure (employing quantitative and qualitative methods to analyze, assess, and track AI risks), and Manage (allocating resources for risk treatment and implementing controls). Each function contains categories and subcategories that organizations can tailor to their context. The NIST AI RMF has been adopted alongside the EU AI Act as a complementary governance instrument, with NIST focusing on risk management processes and the EU AI Act on regulatory compliance. The governance lifecycle in hummbl-governance maps directly to the four NIST functions: agent guardrails and trust tiers implement Govern, the briefing system and health probes implement Map, the evaluation and alerting systems implement Measure, and the kill switch and circuit breaker implement Manage.

entries.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
entries.author: Yao, Shunyu and Zhao, Jeffrey and Yu, Dian and Du, Nan and Shafran, Izhak and Narasimhan, Karthik and Cao, Yuan
entries.abstract: This paper introduces ReAct, a framework that interleaves reasoning traces and task-specific actions in LLMs. By generating verbal reasoning (``thoughts'') alongside actions, the model can create, maintain, and adjust plans while also interacting with external environments to incorporate new information. ReAct outperforms chain-of-thought reasoning alone on knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (ALFWorld, WebShop). The key insight is that reasoning helps the model track progress and handle exceptions, while acting lets it gather information from the external world. Every agent in the founder-mode fleet --- from the briefing scheduler to the forge workers --- implements this think-then-act loop: the coordination bus captures the ``thought'' trace, and tool calls (gh, linear, calendar) execute the ``action'' step.

entries.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
entries.author: Wei, Jason and Wang, Xuezhi and Schuurmans, Dale and Bosma, Maarten and Ichter, Brian and Xia, Fei and Chi, Ed and Le, Quoc V. and Zhou, Denny
entries.abstract: This paper demonstrates that generating a chain of intermediate reasoning steps (chain-of-thought) significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning. Using few-shot prompting with exemplars that include step-by-step reasoning, the authors show substantial improvements on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning benchmarks. The effect is an emergent ability of model scale: chain-of-thought prompting does not help smaller models but produces dramatic gains in models with approximately 100B+ parameters. The paper establishes that the format of prompts --- not just their content --- fundamentally shapes model capabilities. Every prompt in the hummbl-dev ecosystem, from briefing generation to code review to Base120 model application, relies on structured reasoning chains to elicit reliable, auditable outputs from frontier models.

entries.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
entries.author: Lewis, Patrick and Perez, Ethan and Piktus, Aleksandra and Petroni, Fabio and Karpukhin, Vladimir and Goyal, Naman and K\"{u}ttler, Heinrich and Lewis, Mike and Yih, Wen-tau and Rockt\"{a}schel, Tim and Riedel, Sebastian and Kiela, Douwe
entries.abstract: This paper introduces Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), which combines a pre-trained parametric memory (a seq2seq model) with a non-parametric memory (a dense vector index of Wikipedia) accessed via a neural retriever. The model learns to retrieve relevant passages and use them to generate answers, outperforming purely parametric models on knowledge-intensive tasks including open-domain QA, fact verification, and Jeopardy question generation. RAG can be updated without retraining by simply swapping the knowledge index. The Cognitive Ledger Protocol (CLP) in founder-mode implements a form of RAG: the retriever module searches across five memory pools (ledger, bus, briefings, findings, MEMORY.md) using BM25 indexing, and the results are injected as context for the boot\_context session initialization. The consolidator on nodezero periodically synthesizes these retrievals into denser representations.

entries.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
entries.author: Schick, Timo and Dwivedi-Yu, Jane and Dess\`{i}, Roberto and Raileanu, Roberta and Lomeli, Maria and Zettlemoyer, Luke and Cancedda, Nicola and Scialom, Thomas
entries.abstract: Toolformer demonstrates that language models can learn to use external tools (calculators, search engines, calendars, translation systems, Q\&A APIs) in a self-supervised manner by generating API calls within text sequences. The model decides which APIs to call, when to call them, what arguments to pass, and how to incorporate results into its generation --- all without explicit tool-use training data from humans. The key technique is filtering self-generated API calls by whether they reduce perplexity on future tokens.
