This is the theoretical foundation for tool-augmented agents: the MCP server in hummbl-dev exposes Base120 models as a tool API, the GitHub/Linear/Calendar integrations in founder-mode are invoked as tool calls during briefing generation, and the forge workers execute tool calls to accomplish their sub-tasks.

entries.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
entries.author: Liu, Xiao and Yu, Hao and Zhang, Hanchen and Xu, Yifan and Lei, Xuanyu and Lai, Hanyu and Gu, Yu and Ding, Hangliang and Men, Kaiwen and Yang, Kejuan and Zhang, Shudan and Deng, Xiang and Zeng, Aohan and Du, Zhengxiao and Zhang, Chenhui and Shen, Sheng and Zhang, Tianjun and Su, Yu and Sun, Huan and Huang, Minlie and Dong, Yuxiao and Tang, Jie
entries.abstract: AgentBench provides a multi-dimensional benchmark for evaluating LLMs as agents across 8 distinct environments: operating system interaction, database querying, knowledge graph reasoning, digital card games, lateral thinking puzzles, household tasks, web shopping, and web browsing. The benchmark reveals a significant gap between commercial and open-source LLMs in agentic capabilities, with GPT-4 substantially outperforming all other models. The paper establishes that agentic ability is partially orthogonal to traditional NLP benchmarks, meaning models that score well on static tasks may fail at multi-step agent interactions. This is directly relevant to the hybrid-inference routing policy in hummbl-dev: not all models are equal for agentic tasks, and the router must select the right model for the right task rather than defaulting to the cheapest option.

entries.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
entries.author: Jimenez, Carlos E. and Yang, John and Wettig, Alexander and Yao, Shunyu and Pei, Kexin and Press, Ofir and Narasimhan, Karthik R.
entries.abstract: SWE-bench presents 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories. Unlike synthetic coding benchmarks, SWE-bench requires models to understand entire codebases, localize bugs, and produce patches that pass existing test suites. The best models at introduction solved only 1.96\% of tasks, establishing the enormous difficulty of autonomous software engineering. The benchmark has since become the primary measure of agentic coding capability, with specialized systems like SWE-Agent and Devin pushing resolution rates above 30\%. The swarm-test architecture at hummbl-dev (12-terminal parallel Claude swarm) and the arbiter code quality system directly address this problem space: coordinating multiple agents to understand, modify, and validate changes across a large real-world codebase.

entries.title: A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
entries.author: Wang, Lei and Ma, Chen and Feng, Xueyang and Zhang, Zeyu and Yang, Hao and Zhang, Jingsen and Chen, Zhiyuan and Tang, Jiakai and Chen, Xu and Lin, Yankai and Zhao, Wayne Xin and Wei, Zhenhua and Wen, Ji-Rong
entries.abstract: This comprehensive survey reviews the field of LLM-based autonomous agents from three perspectives: construction (profiling, memory, planning, and action modules), application (social simulation, natural science, engineering), and evaluation (subjective, objective, and benchmark-based). The survey identifies four key architectural components that every LLM agent requires: a profile that shapes behavior, a memory system for storing and retrieving experience, a planning mechanism for task decomposition and scheduling, and an action interface for tool use and environment interaction. This four-component framework maps precisely onto the founder-mode agent architecture: agent identity files (profile), the Cognitive Ledger + MEMORY.md (memory), the scheduler and sprint recommender (planning), and the 7 integration adapters (action).

entries.title: GPTSwarm: Language Agents as Optimizable Graphs
entries.author: Zhuge, Mingchen and Wang, Wenyi and Kirsch, Louis and Faccio, Francesco and Khizbullin, Dmitrii and Schmidhuber, J\"{u}rgen
entries.abstract: GPTSwarm represents multi-agent LLM systems as computational graphs where nodes are individual LLM agents and edges represent communication channels. The key contribution is showing that the structure of multi-agent collaboration itself can be optimized: rather than hand-designing which agents talk to which other agents, GPTSwarm uses reinforcement learning to discover optimal graph topologies for specific tasks. The framework supports heterogeneous agents (different models, prompts, and tools) and can dynamically add, remove, or rewire connections based on task performance. Experiments on benchmarks including MMLU, HumanEval, and GSM8K demonstrate that optimized agent graphs outperform both single-agent baselines and fixed multi-agent architectures. The core insight -- that the communication structure between agents is as important as the agents themselves -- directly applies to the hummbl-dev fleet coordination architecture: the TSV bus topology, the agent scope constraints, and the trust-tier hierarchy collectively define a communication graph that shapes emergent fleet behavior. GPTSwarm suggests this graph could be optimized rather than hand-designed.

entries.title: RouteLLM: Learning to Route LLMs with Preference Data
entries.author: Ong, Isaac and Almahairi, Amjad and Wu, Vincent and Chiang, Wei-Lin and Wu, Tianhao and Gonzalez, Joseph E. and Kadous, M Waleed and Stoica, Ion
entries.abstract: RouteLLM trains lightweight router models that dynamically select between a stronger and weaker LLM at inference time, optimizing the cost-quality tradeoff. Using human preference data from Chatbot Arena and data augmentation techniques, the routers learn to predict when a cheaper model will produce a response of comparable quality to an expensive one. The framework achieves over 2x cost reduction without degrading response quality, and the trained routers generalize across model pairs. This work provides the academic foundation for the routing decision that hybrid-inference makes on every query: the policy engine evaluates task requirements against model capabilities and cost, selecting the optimal model from the available fleet (local Ollama, Claude, GPT, etc.).

entries.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
entries.author: Shinn, Noah and Cassano, Federico and Gopinath, Ashwin and Narasimhan, Karthik and Yao, Shunyu
entries.abstract: Reflexion introduces a framework where language agents learn from their mistakes through verbal self-reflection rather than weight updates. After a failed attempt at a task, the agent generates a natural language reflection analyzing what went wrong, and stores this reflection in an episodic memory buffer that informs future attempts. This achieves state-of-the-art results on decision-making (AlfWorld), reasoning (HotpotQA), and programming (HumanEval) tasks. The key insight is that language itself can serve as the reinforcement signal --- no gradient updates required. The Cognitive Ledger Protocol implements this pattern: agents post reflections to the ledger after completing tasks, and boot\_context injects these reflections into future sessions, enabling cross-session learning without model fine-tuning.

entries.title: The Prompt Report: A Systematic Survey of Prompting Techniques
entries.author: Schulhoff, Sander and Ilie, Michael and Balepur, Nishant and Kahadze, Konstantine and Liu, Amanda and Si, Chenglei and Li, Yinheng and Gupta, Aayush and Han, HyoJung and Schulhoff, Sevien and others
entries.abstract: The most comprehensive survey of prompt engineering to date, analyzing 1,565 papers to produce a taxonomy of 58 text-based prompting techniques and 40 techniques for other modalities, along with 33 standardized vocabulary terms. The survey categorizes techniques by function: zero-shot (role prompting, emotion prompting, SimToM), few-shot (chain-of-thought, self-consistency, tree-of-thought), thought generation, decomposition, ensembling, and self-criticism. By establishing prompt engineering as a systematic discipline with reproducible techniques rather than ad-hoc craft, this work provides the theoretical vocabulary for how every agent in the hummbl-dev fleet is instructed: from the apex agent definition to the 130 Claude Code skills, each of which embodies specific prompting patterns from this taxonomy.

entries.title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
entries.author: Wang, Guanzhi and Xie, Yuqi and Jiang, Yunfan and Mandlekar, Ajay and Xiao, Chaowei and Zhu, Yuke and Fan, Linxi and Anandkumar, Anima
entries.abstract: Voyager is the first LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent, demonstrated in Minecraft. It consists of three components: an automatic curriculum that maximizes exploration, an ever-growing skill library of executable code for storing and retrieving complex behaviors, and an iterative prompting mechanism with environment feedback and self-verification. Voyager obtains 3.3x more unique items, travels 2.3x longer distances, and unlocks key tech tree milestones up to 15.3x faster than prior methods --- all without gradient-based training. The skill library pattern --- where successful solutions are stored as reusable code and retrieved by semantic similarity for future tasks --- is implemented in the Claude Code skills system (130 skills) and the forge worker library, where successful task patterns are captured and reused across sessions.

entries.title: Language Agent Tree Search Unifies Reasoning, Acting, and Planning in Language Models
entries.author: Zhou, Andy and Yan, Kai and Shlapentokh-Rothman, Michal and Wang, Haohan and Wang, Yu-Xiong
entries.abstract: LATS (Language Agent Tree Search) unifies reasoning, acting, and planning by using LLMs as agents, value functions, and optimizers within a Monte Carlo Tree Search framework. The agent generates multiple reasoning-action trajectories as a search tree, uses the LLM to evaluate intermediate states (value function), and backpropagates these evaluations to guide future exploration. This enables systematic exploration of the action space with self-reflection at each node. LATS achieves state-of-the-art results on HumanEval (94.4\%), WebShop (75.9\%), and HotpotQA by combining the strengths of ReAct-style action generation with tree search planning. The key insight is that LLMs can serve as both the policy (generating actions) and the critic (evaluating outcomes), eliminating the need for external reward models. For hummbl-dev, LATS formalizes the pattern behind multi-agent task decomposition: the forge orchestrator explores multiple implementation strategies (tree branches), evaluates intermediate results (test outcomes, lint results), and backtracks when a strategy fails -- implementing tree search over the engineering action space.

entries.title: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
entries.author: Patil, Shishir G. and Zhang, Tianjun and Wang, Xin and Gonzalez, Joseph E.
entries.abstract: Gorilla addresses a critical challenge for LLM agents: accurately generating API calls from natural language instructions. The paper demonstrates that LLMs frequently hallucinate API parameters, confuse similar APIs, and generate syntactically correct but semantically wrong calls. Gorilla fine-tunes LLaMA on a dataset of 1,645 APIs from TorchHub, TensorHub, and HuggingFace, combined with a retrieval-aware training approach that conditions generation on API documentation retrieved at inference time. The retriever-aware training is key: it teaches the model to use retrieved documentation as context rather than relying on memorized (and potentially outdated) API signatures. Gorilla achieves higher accuracy than GPT-4 on API call generation while significantly reducing hallucination. For hummbl-dev, Gorilla validates the MCP architecture: by providing structured API documentation (tool descriptions, parameter schemas) at inference time rather than relying on the model's parametric memory, MCP reduces tool-call hallucination -- the same retrieval-augmented approach that Gorilla demonstrates is superior to memorization.

entries.title: MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
entries.author: Packer, Charles and Wooders, Sarah and Lin, Kevin and Fang, Vivian and Patil, Shishir G. and Stoica, Ion and Gonzalez, Joseph E.
entries.abstract: MemGPT introduces a virtual context management system inspired by operating system memory hierarchies. Just as an OS pages data between fast RAM and slow disk, MemGPT manages an LLM's limited context window by paging information between a main context (the model's active context window) and an external storage (a database or file system). The system uses function calls to manage its own memory: it can push information to archival storage, retrieve it later, and manage a FIFO buffer for recent conversation history. This enables LLMs to handle tasks requiring effectively unbounded context -- analyzing long documents, maintaining persistent conversational state across sessions, and building up knowledge over extended interactions. For hummbl-dev, MemGPT provides the theoretical model for the Cognitive Ledger Protocol's memory architecture: the boot context injection (main context), the five-pool retriever (retrieval from archival storage), and the BM25 index (the page table) implement the same memory hierarchy -- managing what fits in an agent's context window versus what is stored in the ledger for on-demand retrieval.

entries.title: DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines
entries.author: Khattab, Omar and Singhvi, Arnav and Maheshwari, Paridhi and Zhang, Zhiyuan and Santhanam, Keshav and Vardhamanan, Sri and Haq, Saiful and Sharma, Ashutosh and Joshi, Thomas T. and Mober, Hanna and Shah, Pawan Kumar and others
entries.abstract: DSPy replaces hand-written prompts with declarative modules that are compiled into optimized prompt chains through automatic prompt tuning. The programmer defines signatures (input-output specifications like ''question -> answer''), modules (compositions of signatures with control flow), and metrics (evaluation functions). The DSPy compiler then optimizes the pipeline by: generating few-shot examples from a training set, selecting optimal demonstrations via bootstrapping, and tuning instructions through self-refinement. This transforms prompt engineering from a manual craft into a systematic, reproducible engineering discipline. DSPy programs outperform hand-tuned prompts on multiple benchmarks while being more maintainable and adaptable to model changes. For hummbl-dev, DSPy's declarative approach aligns with the contract-driven architecture: just as DSPy separates the ''what'' (signatures) from the ''how'' (compiled prompts), the contract schemas in founder-mode separate interface specifications from their implementation, enabling the same governance logic to work across different LLM backends.

entries.title: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation
entries.author: Wu, Qingyun and Bansal, Gagan and Zhang, Jieyu and Wu, Yiran and Li, Beibin and Zhu, Erkang and Jiang, Li and Zhang, Xiaoyun and Zhang, Shaokun and Liu, Jiale and Awadallah, Ahmed Hassan and White, Ryen W. and Burger, Doug and Wang, Chi
entries.abstract: AutoGen introduces a multi-agent conversation framework where customizable agents communicate through natural language messages to collaboratively solve tasks. The framework supports three agent types: AssistantAgent (LLM-backed), UserProxyAgent (human-in-the-loop or code-executing), and custom agents. Key design principles include conversable agents (any agent can send and receive messages), customizable conversation patterns (sequential, group chat, hierarchical), and flexible human involvement (from fully autonomous to human-in-the-loop at every step). AutoGen enables complex workflows like multi-agent coding (one agent writes, another reviews), debate-based problem solving (agents argue different perspectives), and retrieval-augmented generation with specialized agents. For hummbl-dev, AutoGen's architecture closely parallels the multi-agent coordination model: the TSV bus provides the message-passing substrate (analogous to AutoGen's conversation channel), agent guardrails define the conversation patterns (who can communicate what to whom), and the trust tier system controls human involvement (owner approval for destructive actions, autonomous execution within approved scopes).

entries.title: Improving Factuality and Reasoning in Language Models through Multiagent Debate
entries.author: Du, Yilun and Li, Shuang and Torralba, Antonio and Tenenbaum, Joshua B. and Mordatch, Igor
entries.abstract: This paper demonstrates that having multiple LLM instances debate each other significantly improves factuality and reasoning accuracy. In the debate framework, multiple model instances independently generate responses, then engage in rounds of debate where each instance can see the others' responses and is asked to critically evaluate and revise its own answer. After several rounds, a final answer is determined by consensus. The approach improves performance on mathematical reasoning (GSM8K), strategic reasoning (chess), and factual accuracy (Biography Generation) benchmarks. The key insight is that individual LLMs have correlated error patterns that debate helps identify and correct through adversarial cross-examination. For hummbl-dev, the multi-agent debate pattern is partially implemented through the PROPOSAL/ACK cycle on the coordination bus: when one agent proposes a change, other agents (or the human operator) can challenge it before it is committed. The agent audit process for Gemini sessions is a formalized debate where Claude critically evaluates Gemini's output before integration.

entries.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
entries.author: Yao, Shunyu and Yu, Dian and Zhao, Jeffrey and Shafran, Izhak and Griffiths, Tom L. and Cao, Yuan and Narasimhan, Karthik
entries.abstract: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) generalizes chain-of-thought prompting by enabling LLMs to explore multiple reasoning paths as a tree structure, evaluate intermediate thoughts, and make global decisions about which branches to pursue using search algorithms (BFS or DFS). Unlike chain-of-thought which commits to a single linear reasoning path, ToT maintains a set of candidate partial solutions, generates possible next steps for each, evaluates their promise using the LLM itself, and prunes unpromising branches. This enables lookahead, backtracking, and strategic exploration -- capabilities absent from linear prompting. ToT achieves dramatic improvements on tasks requiring planning and search, including Game of 24 (74\% vs 4\% for CoT) and creative writing. For hummbl-dev, ToT formalizes the deliberation pattern used in sprint planning: the sprint recommender explores multiple potential sprint configurations (tree branches), evaluates each against priority criteria and resource constraints (thought evaluation), and selects the most promising configuration -- applying tree search to organizational planning rather than mathematical reasoning.

entries.title: Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents
entries.author: Sumers, Theodore R. and Yao, Shunyu and Narasimhan, Karthik and Griffiths, Thomas L.
entries.abstract: This paper proposes CoALA (Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents), a conceptual framework that organizes the design space of language agent architectures by drawing on cognitive science principles. CoALA decomposes agents into three components: memory (working memory for in-context state, episodic memory for past experiences, semantic memory for world knowledge, and procedural memory for action policies), action space (internal actions like reasoning and retrieval, and external actions like tool use and communication), and decision-making (how the agent selects among actions, ranging from simple prompting to learned policies to explicit planning). The framework reveals that most existing language agents implement narrow subsets of this design space and identifies underexplored combinations -- such as agents with learned retrieval policies over episodic memory, or agents that use planning over internal reasoning actions. CoALA provides a systematic vocabulary for comparing architectures: ReAct uses working memory with alternating internal-external actions, Voyager adds procedural memory for skill accumulation, and MemGPT implements explicit memory management. For hummbl-dev, the CoALA taxonomy maps precisely to the Cognitive Ledger Protocol architecture: the CLP's five memory pools (ledger, bus, briefings, findings, MEMORY.md) implement episodic, semantic, and working memory; the seven adapters implement the external action space; and the scheduler/sprint-recommender implements the decision-making module.

entries.title: The Nature of Explanation
entries.author: Craik, Kenneth J. W.
entries.abstract: This posthumously influential work is the origin of the mental models concept in cognitive science. Craik proposed that the mind constructs ``small-scale models'' of reality to anticipate events, reason about alternatives, and generate explanations. He argued that thought proceeds in three stages: translation of external processes into internal representations, derivation of new symbols through inferential manipulation, and re-translation back to external actions. The brain, Craik argued, is fundamentally a modeling machine that parallels external processes via internal symbolic structures. This is the deepest root of the Base120 framework: 120 mental models organized as cognitive tools for six fundamental transformations (P, IN, CO, DE, RE, SY) --- a systematic operationalization of Craik's thesis that humans think by constructing and manipulating models of the world.

entries.title: Mental Models
entries.author: Gentner, Dedre and Stevens, Albert L.
entries.abstract: This edited volume established mental models as a rigorous interdisciplinary research program by bringing together cognitive psychologists, AI researchers, and education scientists. The contributors demonstrate that people reason about physical, social, and abstract systems by constructing simplified internal models that capture structural relationships rather than surface features. Key chapters cover mental models of physical mechanisms (de Kleer \& Brown), analogical reasoning (Gentner's structure-mapping theory), and expert-novice differences in model construction. The book's central finding --- that the quality of a person's mental model determines the quality of their reasoning --- is the foundational claim behind Base120: by providing high-quality, pre-structured mental models for six cognitive transformations, the framework bootstraps better reasoning than ad-hoc model construction.

entries.title: Metacognition and Cognitive Monitoring: A New Area of Cognitive-Developmental Inquiry
entries.author: Flavell, John H.
entries.abstract: This foundational paper defines metacognition as ``knowledge and cognition about cognitive phenomena'' and proposes a model with four interacting components: metacognitive knowledge (beliefs about persons, tasks, and strategies), metacognitive experiences (conscious feelings during cognition), goals/tasks, and actions/strategies. Flavell distinguishes between knowing about cognition (declarative metacognitive knowledge) and regulating cognition (procedural metacognitive monitoring). The paper established metacognition as a major research program in cognitive and developmental psychology. The convergence guard and reward monitor in hummbl-governance implement computational metacognition: they monitor the agent system's own cognitive processes (Is the fleet converging on an answer? Are reward signals consistent with goals?) and trigger corrective action when metacognitive checks fail --- exactly the self-monitoring loop Flavell describes.

entries.title: Acquisition of Cognitive Skill
entries.author: Anderson, John R.
entries.abstract: This paper introduces knowledge compilation as the mechanism by which cognitive skills transition from slow, declarative processing to fast, proceduralized execution. Anderson's ACT framework posits two stages: a declarative stage (where facts about a domain are interpreted step-by-step) and a procedural stage (where domain knowledge is directly embodied in production rules). Knowledge compilation consists of composition (collapsing sequences of productions into single productions) and procedural embedding (embedding declarative facts directly into procedures). The theory explains why novices are slow and conscious while experts are fast and automatic --- a progression from explicit reasoning to compiled skill. The consolidator module on nodezero implements this exact process: periodically synthesizing verbose ledger entries, bus messages, and findings into denser, compiled knowledge representations that enable faster session bootstrapping.

entries.title: Perception in Chess
entries.author: Chase, William G. and Simon, Herbert A.
entries.abstract: This landmark study demonstrated that chess masters' superior recall of board positions is not due to better memory per se but to their ability to perceive positions as meaningful ``chunks'' --- patterns of pieces that recur across games. Masters recalled positions from real games far better than novices, but showed no advantage for random piece placements, proving that expertise resides in a library of 50,000--100,000 chunks built through years of practice. The chunks are not memorized positions but abstract patterns that enable rapid recognition and response. This is the empirical foundation for Base120's design: the 120 models are pre-compiled cognitive chunks --- recurring patterns of thought (perspective shifts, inversions, compositions, decompositions, recursions, systems views) that practitioners can recognize and deploy without building them from scratch.

entries.title: Cognitive Load During Problem Solving: Effects on Learning
entries.author: Sweller, John
entries.abstract: This paper introduces cognitive load theory, demonstrating that conventional problem solving via means-ends analysis imposes heavy cognitive load that interferes with schema acquisition and learning. Sweller shows that goal-free problems (``calculate as many values as you can'') produce equivalent problem-solving performance but superior learning, because they free working memory resources for schema construction. The theory distinguishes intrinsic load (inherent complexity of the material), extraneous load (imposed by poor instructional design), and germane load (devoted to schema construction). The morning briefing system in founder-mode is a direct application: by compiling overnight events, metrics, and recommendations into a structured daily summary, it reduces the extraneous cognitive load of manually scanning GitHub, Linear, Calendar, and cost dashboards, freeing the founder's working memory for the germane task of strategic decision-making.

entries.title: Organizational Memory
entries.author: Walsh, James P. and Ungson, Gerardo Rivera
entries.abstract: This seminal paper defines organizational memory as stored information from an organization's history that can be brought to bear on present decisions. Walsh and Ungson identify five retention facilities (or ``storage bins'') where organizational memory resides: individuals, culture, transformations (standard operating procedures), structures (organizational roles), and ecology (physical workspace). They model three processes --- acquisition, retention, and retrieval --- and argue that organizational memory is fundamentally distributed: no single person or artifact holds the complete memory. The five-bin model maps directly to how founder-mode stores its organizational memory: individuals (agent identity files), culture (SOUL.md, rules/), transformations (the 130 skills, briefing templates), structures (agent registry, trust tiers), and ecology (the coordination bus, file system layout, briefing archive).

entries.title: Transactive Memory: A Contemporary Analysis of the Group Mind
entries.author: Wegner, Daniel M.
entries.abstract: Wegner introduces transactive memory as a shared system for encoding, storing, and retrieving knowledge that emerges in groups. Rather than each member duplicating all knowledge, group members develop a shared awareness of ``who knows what'' and rely on each other for specialized knowledge retrieval. The transactive memory system has three processes: directory updating (learning what others know), information allocation (routing new information to the right expert), and retrieval coordination (knowing whom to ask). The multi-agent coordination bus in founder-mode is a computational transactive memory system: each agent has specialized knowledge (Claude for integration, Gemini for research, Codex for verification), the bus directory tracks agent capabilities and status, and agents post QUESTION messages to route information requests to the right specialist.

entries.title: A Dynamic Theory of Organizational Knowledge Creation
entries.author: Nonaka, Ikujiro
entries.abstract: This paper presents the SECI model of knowledge creation, describing four modes of conversion between tacit and explicit knowledge: Socialization (tacit to tacit, through shared experience), Externalization (tacit to explicit, through articulation and conceptualization), Combination (explicit to explicit, through systematization), and Internalization (explicit to tacit, through learning-by-doing). Knowledge creation occurs as a spiral through these four modes, amplifying from individual to group to organizational levels. The Cognitive Ledger Protocol implements the SECI spiral computationally: agents externalize tacit session knowledge into ledger entries (Externalization), the consolidator combines entries into synthesized knowledge (Combination), boot\_context injects this knowledge into new sessions (Internalization), and the coordination bus enables agents to share context without explicit articulation (Socialization).

entries.title: Distributed Cognition: Toward a New Foundation for Human-Computer Interaction Research
entries.author: Hollan, James D. and Hutchins, Edwin and Kirsh, David
entries.abstract: This paper extends distributed cognition from a theory of team coordination (Hutchins 1995, already in T2) into a design framework for human-computer interaction. The authors argue that cognition is not confined to individual minds but is distributed across people, artifacts, and time. They identify three types of distribution: across members of a social group, between internal and external representations, and through time (where earlier events shape later ones). The paper proposes that HCI should study cognitive systems holistically rather than focusing on the individual user-computer dyad. The founder-mode architecture embodies all three distributions: cognition is distributed across the agent fleet (social), between agent memory and the coordination bus (internal/external), and across sessions via the Cognitive Ledger and briefing archive (temporal).

entries.title: Cognitive Artifacts
entries.author: Norman, Donald A.
entries.journal: In J.M. Carroll (Ed.), Designing Interaction: Psychology at the Human-Computer Interface
entries.abstract: Norman defines cognitive artifacts as artificial devices designed to maintain, display, or operate upon information in order to serve a representational function. He draws a crucial distinction between the ``personal view'' (how an artifact changes the task for its user) and the ``system view'' (how it changes the task as a whole, often redistributing cognitive effort rather than eliminating it). A cognitive artifact does not make a person smarter; it transforms the task so that less cognitive effort is required for the human part, often by offloading memory or computation to the artifact. The entire hummbl-dev toolkit is a system of cognitive artifacts in Norman's sense: the morning briefing transforms the founder's task from ``scan 7 data sources'' to ``read a structured summary''; the Base120 models transform ``build a mental model from scratch'' to ``select and apply a pre-built model''; the coordination bus transforms ``remember what every agent did'' to ``read the latest messages.'

entries.title: Kommunikation mit Zettelk\"{a}sten: Ein Erfahrungsbericht
entries.author: Luhmann, Niklas
entries.journal: In H. Baier et al. (Eds.), \"{O}ffentliche Meinung und sozialer Wandel / Public Opinion and Social Change
entries.abstract: In this essay, Luhmann describes his Zettelkasten (``slip box'') as a ``communication partner'' --- a system of 90,000 interlinked index cards that functioned not merely as a filing system but as a thinking tool that generated unexpected connections and new ideas. The key innovation is the numbering and linking scheme: each note receives a unique hierarchical address, and notes link to related notes regardless of where they sit in the hierarchy, enabling non-linear traversal of ideas. Luhmann credits this system for his extraordinary productivity (50 books, 550+ articles). The Cognitive Ledger Protocol in founder-mode explicitly implements Zettelkasten principles: each LedgerEntry has a unique ID, entries link to related entries via the ``links'' field (Zettelkasten-style cross-references), and the BM25 indexer enables retrieval by semantic similarity --- a computational version of Luhmann's browsing-by-following-links.

entries.title: The Origins of Intelligence in Children
entries.author: Piaget, Jean
entries.abstract: Piaget introduces the concepts of schema, assimilation, and accommodation as the fundamental mechanisms of cognitive development. A schema is an organized pattern of thought or action that represents knowledge about the world; assimilation is the process of incorporating new information into existing schemas; accommodation is the modification of schemas when new information cannot be assimilated. Intelligence develops through repeated cycles of assimilation-accommodation that produce increasingly abstract and coordinated schemas. Piaget's schema theory is the deepest theoretical root of Base120. Each of the 120 mental models is a pre-constructed schema --- an organized pattern of thought for a specific cognitive transformation (perspective shift, inversion, composition, decomposition, recursion, or systems view). When a practitioner applies a Base120 model to a new problem, they are performing Piagetian assimilation: fitting the problem into an existing schema. When a model proves insufficient and must be adapted, they perform accommodation. The consolidator module on nodezero automates schema refinement --- synthesizing verbose ledger entries into denser representations that are better adapted to the problem domain.

entries.title: Remembering: A Study in Experimental and Social Psychology
entries.author: Bartlett, Frederic C.
entries.abstract: Bartlett's landmark experimental work demonstrates that memory is not a passive recording of experience but an active, constructive process shaped by prior knowledge structures he called ``schemas.'' Through serial reproduction experiments (most famously ``The War of the Ghosts''), Bartlett showed that people systematically distort recalled material to fit their existing schemas --- omitting unfamiliar details, rationalizing inconsistencies, and importing culturally familiar elements. Memory is reconstruction, not reproduction. This finding has profound implications for the Cognitive Ledger Protocol and the multi-agent coordination bus. When agents post SITREP messages, they are reconstructing their session activity through the lens of their operational schema --- and Bartlett predicts systematic distortion. The Gemini audit history confirms this empirically: Gemini's metric inflation (17x in Session 7) is classic schema-driven reconstruction, where ``what I did'' is distorted toward ``what I expected to accomplish.'' The append-only ledger design is a defense against Bartlettian distortion --- it preserves the raw record alongside any synthesized summaries, preventing reconstructive memory from overwriting the original data.

entries.title: Situated Learning: Legitimate Peripheral Participation
entries.author: Lave, Jean and Wenger, Etienne
entries.abstract: Lave and Wenger reconceptualize learning as a process of increasing participation in communities of practice rather than the acquisition of propositional knowledge. Newcomers begin as ``legitimate peripheral participants'' --- performing simple, low-risk tasks at the edges of a community --- and gradually move toward full participation as they develop competence and identity within the practice. Learning is inseparable from the social context in which it occurs. The hummbl-dev agent fleet implements legitimate peripheral participation for AI agents. New agents (Kimi, Gemini) begin with constrained scope --- peripheral participation limited to specific directories and task types. As trust develops through successful audits, scope expands. Gemini's trajectory illustrates the theory's dark side: it attempted to skip from peripheral to full participation (committing to main, modifying core services) without the gradual trust-building that legitimate peripheral participation requires, triggering the probation and guardrail system. The agent onboarding protocol is a formalized version of Lave and Wenger's apprenticeship model --- new agents learn the community's norms (commit conventions, bus protocol, scope rules) through bounded practice.

entries.title: Communities of Practice: Learning, Meaning, and Identity
entries.author: Wenger, Etienne
entries.abstract: Wenger extends the concept of communities of practice into a full social theory of learning organized around four components: meaning (learning as experience), practice (learning as doing), community (learning as belonging), and identity (learning as becoming). A community of practice is defined by three dimensions: mutual engagement (members do things together), joint enterprise (members negotiate a shared understanding of what they are about), and shared repertoire (members develop routines, artifacts, vocabulary, and styles). The hummbl-dev agent fleet is a community of practice by Wenger's definition. Mutual engagement: agents interact through the coordination bus, producing and consuming shared artifacts (briefings, ledger entries, bus messages). Joint enterprise: the negotiated enterprise is the Morning Briefing --- all agents contribute toward producing a daily decision-support artifact. Shared repertoire: the fleet has developed conventions (TSV bus format, commit message style, scope rules, guardrail documents) that constitute a shared practice. The Cognitive Ledger Protocol reifies the shared repertoire into a persistent, searchable knowledge base.

entries.title: The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance
entries.author: Ericsson, K. Anders and Krampe, Ralf Th. and Tesch-R\"{o}mer, Clemens
entries.abstract: Ericsson, Krampe, and Tesch-R\"{o}mer demonstrate that expert performance is primarily the result of deliberate practice --- effortful activities specifically designed to improve performance --- rather than innate talent. Across domains (music, chess, sports), they find that experts accumulate roughly 10,000 hours of deliberate practice by age 20, and that the quality of practice (targeted, feedback-rich, at the edge of current ability) matters more than quantity. Deliberate practice requires concentration, is not inherently enjoyable, and demands access to teachers or structured learning materials. Base120 is a deliberate practice infrastructure for thinking. Each of the 120 mental models provides a structured exercise at the edge of current analytical ability --- applying perspective shifts, inversions, compositions, and decompositions that practitioners would not naturally attempt. The feedback loops in founder-mode (signal replies, briefing quality ratings, sprint retrospectives) provide the performance feedback that Ericsson identifies as essential. The graduated agent guardrails implement deliberate practice for AI agents: constrained scope forces agents to practice within their competence boundary, expanding only as measured performance improves.

entries.title: Sense-Making Theory and Practice: An Overview of User Interests in Knowledge Seeking and Use
entries.author: Dervin, Brenda
entries.abstract: Dervin's Sense-Making methodology reconceptualizes information seeking as a process of bridging cognitive gaps rather than finding pre-existing answers. The central metaphor is a person moving through time-space who encounters a gap (a situation where current knowledge is insufficient), constructs a bridge (information, ideas, resources), and arrives at an outcome. Sense-Making treats information not as a commodity to be transmitted but as a construction created by the user in context. The Morning Briefing system in founder-mode is a sense-making support tool in Dervin's framework. The founder encounters daily cognitive gaps: What happened overnight? Which projects are on track? Where are the risks? The briefing bridges these gaps by aggregating signals from seven adapters into a structured summary. The Cognitive Ledger Protocol extends sense-making across sessions: when an agent encounters a gap in the current session, the retriever searches the ledger for prior bridges (past entries that addressed similar gaps), implementing what Dervin calls ``circling'' --- revisiting and reinterpreting prior sense-making in light of new situations.

entries.title: Information Foraging
entries.author: Pirolli, Peter and Card, Stuart
entries.abstract: Pirolli and Card apply optimal foraging theory from behavioral ecology to human information-seeking behavior. Just as animals maximize energy intake per unit time by following ``information scent'' (cues that signal the proximity of food), knowledge workers maximize the value of information found per unit time by following ``information scent'' (cues in link text, headings, and summaries that signal the relevance of a source). The theory predicts that people will ``patch'' between information sources, spending time in a patch until diminishing returns make it more efficient to move to the next patch, and that stronger scent leads to more efficient foraging. The BM25 inverted term index in the Cognitive Ledger Protocol is an information scent amplifier: it surfaces the most relevant ledger entries for a given query, increasing the scent on high-value patches. The Morning Briefing itself is a pre-foraged information patch: rather than requiring the founder to forage across seven data sources (high cost, weak scent), the briefing pre-aggregates and ranks the most relevant signals, dramatically reducing foraging time and increasing the scent-to-noise ratio.

entries.title: Cognitive Flexibility, Constructivism, and Hypertext: Random Access Instruction for Advanced Knowledge Acquisition in Ill-Structured Domains
entries.author: Spiro, Rand J. and Feltovich, Paul J. and Jacobson, Michael J. and Coulson, Richard L.
entries.journal: Constructivism and the Technology of Instruction: A Conversation
entries.abstract: Spiro and colleagues introduce cognitive flexibility theory (CFT), arguing that learning in ill-structured domains (medicine, law, literary criticism) requires multiple representations of knowledge rather than a single ``correct'' schema. Ill-structured domains resist the neat categorical organization that works for well-structured domains; instead, learners must develop the flexibility to assemble knowledge adaptively from multiple cases, perspectives, and conceptual dimensions. CFT prescribes ``criss-crossing'' the conceptual landscape from multiple directions, using the same cases to illustrate different themes. Base120's six transformations (P, IN, CO, DE, RE, SY) are a systematic implementation of cognitive flexibility. Each transformation forces the practitioner to criss-cross the same problem from a different conceptual direction --- viewing it through perspective shifts, then inversions, then compositions, then decompositions. The 120 models span 20 models per transformation, ensuring that even within a single cognitive direction there are multiple representations. The Zettelkasten-style links in the Cognitive Ledger enable the non-linear traversal that CFT prescribes --- entries link to related entries across categories, supporting the random-access knowledge assembly that flexible cognition requires.

entries.title: The Nature of the Firm
entries.author: Coase, Ronald H.
entries.abstract: Coase's foundational paper asks why firms exist at all when market transactions could coordinate production. His answer -- transaction costs -- is the intellectual ancestor of every make-vs-buy decision in the hummbl-dev stack. The CostGovernorBridge enforces budget caps on API calls precisely because the cost of using external ML prediction markets (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) can exceed the cost of internal coordination. The delegation token system mirrors Coase's insight that hierarchical authority within a firm reduces negotiation overhead: rather than re-negotiating access rights per request, a signed HMAC token delegates bounded capability once. The GaaS revenue thesis itself is a Coasean proposition -- governance as a service reduces the transaction costs other builders face when coordinating multi-agent systems, creating a firm-like boundary around trust and compliance that the open market cannot efficiently provide.

entries.title: The Economic Institutions of Capitalism: Firms, Markets, Relational Contracting
entries.author: Williamson, Oliver E.
entries.abstract: Williamson extends Coase's transaction cost framework into a full theory of institutional governance, introducing asset specificity, bounded rationality, and opportunism as the key variables determining whether transactions are organized via markets, hierarchies, or hybrid forms. The hummbl-dev circuit breaker pattern is a direct implementation of Williamson's governance switching: when an external adapter's failure rate crosses a threshold (asset specificity of the integration becomes high), the system shifts from market-like API calls to hierarchical fallback (mock data, cached results). The contract-net protocol in multi-agent coordination similarly reflects Williamson's hybrid governance -- agents bid for tasks in a market-like auction but operate under hierarchical kill-switch authority. The frozen contract schemas in the contracts/ directory embody relational contracting: they set stable terms that reduce renegotiation costs across service boundaries.

entries.title: Platform Competition in Two-Sided Markets
entries.author: Rochet, Jean-Charles and Tirole, Jean
entries.journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
entries.abstract: Rochet and Tirole formalize the economics of two-sided platforms, showing how a platform must manage cross-group externalities by subsidizing one side to attract the other. GaaS (Governance-as-a-Service) is structurally a two-sided market: on one side, AI agent builders need governance primitives (circuit breakers, delegation tokens, kill switches); on the other, enterprises need assurance that agents comply with safety contracts. The platform's pricing problem -- whether to charge builders, enterprises, or both -- maps directly to Rochet-Tirole's framework. Their finding that the socially optimal price structure differs from the profit-maximizing one informs the open-source-core-plus-hosted-governance business model. The Base120 API itself exhibits network effects: more mental models in the registry make the platform more valuable to every user, creating the cross-side externality that defines a two-sided market.

entries.title: Two-Sided Network Effects: A Theory of Information Product Design
entries.author: Parker, Geoffrey G. and Van Alstyne, Marshall W.
entries.abstract: Parker and Van Alstyne model how platforms can profitably give away products by exploiting two-sided network effects, where the value of the platform to one side increases with participation on the other. This directly informs GaaS pricing strategy: the Base120 mental model library and MCP server can be offered free to developers (the subsidy side) while enterprises pay for governance compliance dashboards and audit trails (the money side). The paper's analysis of information product design -- specifically the trade-off between openness and control -- maps to the hummbl-dev decision to open-source Base120 while keeping the governance bus append-only and HMAC-signed. Their finding that platforms should subsidize the side with greater cross-network externalities validates the developer-first strategy, since each new agent builder increases the governance surface area that enterprises must monitor.

entries.title: Information Rules: A Strategic Guide to the Network Economy
entries.author: Shapiro, Carl and Varian, Hal R.
entries.abstract: Shapiro and Varian apply classical microeconomics to information goods, establishing that information has high fixed costs of production and near-zero marginal costs of reproduction. This cost structure is the foundation of the entire hummbl-dev economic model: governance rules, mental model schemas, and contract definitions are information goods with exactly this cost profile. Once the Base120 ontology is created, distributing it via API or MCP costs effectively nothing. The book's treatment of lock-in and switching costs explains why the frozen contract baseline (fm-contracts-v0.1) is strategically important -- it creates switching costs for adopters who build against the schema. Their analysis of versioning and bundling informs the tiered GaaS offering: a free tier for individual developers, a compliance tier for startups, and an enterprise tier with audit trails and SLA guarantees.

entries.title: Counterspeculation, Auctions, and Competitive Sealed Tenders
entries.author: Vickrey, William
entries.abstract: Vickrey's seminal paper establishes the theoretical foundation for auction mechanism design, proving that a second-price sealed-bid auction incentivizes truthful bidding. The contract-net protocol used in multi-agent task allocation is a direct descendant of auction mechanisms: agents submit bids for tasks, and the allocator selects based on cost, capability, and availability. Vickrey's truthful revelation principle is relevant to the hummbl-dev delegation token system -- when an agent receives a bounded capability token, the mechanism must ensure the agent has no incentive to misrepresent its capacity or resource consumption. The CostGovernorBridge's budget enforcement can be understood as a modified auction where each API call competes for budget allocation, and the governor acts as the auctioneer enforcing reserve prices (soft caps) and absolute limits (hard caps) to prevent the winner's curse of runaway inference costs.

entries.title: Are We Approaching an Economic Singularity? Information Technology and the Future of Economic Growth
entries.author: Nordhaus, William D.
entries.abstract: Nobel laureate Nordhaus develops a growth model to test whether rapid improvements in computation and AI will trigger an economic singularity -- a regime of ever-accelerating growth. He finds no evidence of singularity in historical data but identifies the conditions under which it could occur, particularly if AI substitutes for human labor across all tasks. This paper provides the macroeconomic context for hummbl-dev's cost governance thesis: if AI inference costs continue their observed exponential decline (280x reduction for GPT-3.5-level performance in two years), budget enforcement systems must be adaptive, not static. The CostGovernorBridge's soft and hard caps are an engineering response to exactly the price dynamics Nordhaus models. His framework also grounds the runway forecasting skill -- projecting when budget constraints will bind requires understanding the deflationary trajectory of AI compute costs.

entries.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
entries.author: Kaplan, Jared and McCandlish, Sam and Henighan, Tom and Brown, Tom B. and Chess, Benjamin and Child, Rewon and Gray, Scott and Radford, Alec and Wu, Jeffrey and Amodei, Dario
entries.abstract: Kaplan et al. discover that neural language model performance follows power-law relationships with model size, dataset size, and compute budget, with trends spanning seven orders of magnitude. This paper is the empirical foundation for the economic logic of the hummbl-dev cost governor. The scaling laws imply that doubling performance requires roughly 10x more compute -- making cost optimization not just engineering housekeeping but a strategic constraint. The CostGovernorBridge's budget enforcement operates in the regime these laws predict: choosing between a large expensive model and a small cheap one is a scaling-law-informed cost-quality tradeoff. The FrugalGPT-style LLM cascade that the RouteLLM integration enables is directly motivated by these findings -- route easy queries to small models (low on the scaling curve) and hard queries to large models (high on the curve), optimizing the cost-quality frontier.

entries.title: {FrugalGPT}: How to Use Large Language Models While Reducing Cost and Improving Performance
entries.author: Chen, Lingjiao and Zaharia, Matei and Zou, James
entries.abstract: FrugalGPT introduces three strategies for reducing LLM inference costs -- prompt adaptation, LLM approximation, and LLM cascade -- demonstrating that matching GPT-4 performance is possible with up to 98 percent cost reduction. This paper is the direct academic antecedent of the hummbl-dev cost governance architecture. The CostGovernorBridge implements budget-constrained API selection; the RouteLLM integration routes queries across model tiers based on complexity. FrugalGPT's LLM cascade strategy -- trying cheap models first, escalating to expensive ones only when confidence is low -- maps precisely to the circuit breaker pattern: if the cheap model fails (low confidence), the breaker opens to the next tier. The paper's empirical finding that model heterogeneity creates arbitrage opportunities validates the multi-adapter architecture in integrations/, where github_adapter, linear_adapter, and ollama_adapter can each route to different cost tiers depending on query complexity and budget remaining.

entries.title: The Rapid Adoption of Data-Driven Decision-Making
entries.author: Brynjolfsson, Erik and McElheran, Kristina
entries.abstract: Using Census Bureau data from a representative sample of U.S. manufacturing plants, Brynjolfsson and McElheran find that data-driven decision-making (DDD) tripled from 11 to 30 percent between 2005 and 2010, with adopters showing 4-8 percent higher productivity. This paper provides the empirical economic justification for the Morning Briefing system -- the core product of hummbl-dev. The briefing aggregates signals from GitHub, Linear, Google Calendar, cost tracking, and security scans into a daily decision-support artifact, precisely the kind of DDD infrastructure that drives the productivity gains measured here. Their finding that DDD adoption correlates with IT investment and educated workers describes the target market for GaaS: technically sophisticated teams with existing infrastructure who need governance tooling to extract full value from their AI investments.

entries.title: Economic Policy for Artificial Intelligence
entries.author: Agrawal, Ajay and Gans, Joshua and Goldfarb, Avi
entries.abstract: Agrawal, Gans, and Goldfarb frame AI as a drop in the cost of prediction, then analyze the policy implications across labor markets, data governance, and industry structure. Their prediction-cost framework explains why the hummbl-dev cost governor is architecturally central rather than an afterthought: if AI is cheap prediction, then governance is the cost of ensuring predictions are safe, compliant, and cost-bounded. The paper's analysis of principal-agent problems in AI delegation -- where the AI agent has private information about its confidence and the principal cannot fully monitor -- directly motivates the HMAC-signed delegation tokens in the IDP system. Their discussion of data as a barrier to entry informs the append-only governance bus design: the accumulated audit trail becomes a competitive moat, since compliance history cannot be replicated by new entrants.

entries.title: Software Engineering for Machine Learning: A Case Study
entries.author: Amershi, Saleema and Begel, Andrew and Bird, Christian and DeLine, Robert and Gall, Harald and Kamar, Ece and Nagappan, Nachiappan and Nushi, Besmira and Zimmermann, Thomas
entries.abstract: Based on interviews with software teams at Microsoft building AI applications, Amershi et al. identify a nine-stage ML workflow and document the engineering challenges of integrating ML into production systems. Their finding that ML systems require fundamentally different testing, monitoring, and deployment practices than traditional software validates the hummbl-dev architecture's investment in specialized infrastructure: the circuit breaker wraps all ML-dependent adapters because ML services fail differently than deterministic APIs. The paper's observation that data management and model evolution create ongoing maintenance costs -- echoing Sculley's ML technical debt -- justifies the cost tracker integration and budget enforcement. Their emphasis on continuous model evaluation maps to the health probe system, where each adapter's reliability is monitored independently and failures trigger governance responses rather than silent degradation.

entries.title: A Theory of Auctions and Competitive Bidding
entries.author: Milgrom, Paul R. and Weber, Robert J.
entries.abstract: Milgrom and Weber develop a general theory of competitive auctions that unifies English, Dutch, first-price sealed-bid, and second-price sealed-bid formats under a common analytical framework. They introduce the concept of ``affiliation'' --- a form of positive statistical dependence among bidders' valuations --- and prove the revenue-ranking theorem: English auctions generate the highest expected revenue when bidders' valuations are affiliated, because the open ascending format reveals information that reduces the winner's curse. The paper establishes that mechanism design choices have real economic consequences. The hummbl-dev multi-agent task allocation implements an auction-like mechanism: when a DIRECTIVE is posted on the bus with multiple lanes, agents implicitly bid for tasks based on capability and availability. The dispatch system's allocation logic faces exactly the valuation uncertainty Milgrom and Weber analyze --- the operator does not know each agent's true capability for a given task. The agent guardrails serve as reserve prices: agents below a trust threshold cannot bid on sensitive tasks, preventing adverse selection in task allocation.

entries.title: Firms, Contracts, and Financial Structure
entries.author: Hart, Oliver
entries.abstract: Hart develops the incomplete contracts framework, arguing that contracts cannot specify actions for every conceivable contingency, and that residual control rights --- the authority to make decisions in uncontracted situations --- determine the boundaries of the firm. The key insight is that ownership matters precisely because contracts are incomplete: whoever holds residual control rights shapes outcomes in unforeseen circumstances. This theory directly explains the hummbl-dev governance architecture. The frozen contract schemas in contracts/ are intentionally incomplete --- they specify message formats, data models, and interface boundaries but cannot anticipate every agent behavior. The residual control rights reside with the human operator (kill switch authority, DIRECTIVE messages) and the audit gate (Claude review before merge). The delegation token system implements Hart's framework computationally: a token grants specific, bounded authority (the contracted portion) while the issuer retains residual control via token revocation and chain depth limits (the uncontracted portion).

entries.title: The Market for ``Lemons'': Quality Uncertainty and the Market Mechanism
entries.author: Akerlof, George A.
entries.abstract: Akerlof demonstrates that information asymmetry --- where sellers know product quality but buyers do not --- can cause market failure through adverse selection. In the used car market, buyers who cannot distinguish good cars from ``lemons'' offer a price reflecting average quality, which drives out good-car sellers, leaving only lemons. The resulting ``market for lemons'' can collapse entirely. Information asymmetry is the fundamental challenge of multi-agent AI coordination. When an agent posts a SITREP to the bus claiming ``5 files, 334 lines,'' the human operator cannot immediately verify whether this is a high-quality contribution or a lemon. The Gemini audit history documents exactly this dynamic: Gemini's inflated metrics (17x in Session 7) are lemons disguised as quality. The hummbl-dev response implements Akerlof's prescribed solutions: signaling (agents must provide verifiable evidence, not just claims), screening (the audit gate inspects actual diffs, not self-reports), and reputation (the guardrails track trust across sessions, creating a quality history that reduces information asymmetry over time).

entries.title: Moral Hazard and Observability
entries.author: Holmstr\"{o}m, Bengt
entries.abstract: Holmstr\"{o}m formalizes the moral hazard problem in principal-agent relationships: when an agent's effort is unobservable, the agent may shirk because the principal cannot distinguish low effort from bad luck. He proves the informativeness principle --- any signal that is informative about the agent's effort should be included in the optimal contract, because additional information always (weakly) improves the principal-agent outcome. This paper is the theoretical foundation for the hummbl-dev agent monitoring infrastructure. The human operator (principal) cannot directly observe agent effort --- only outputs (code commits, bus messages, test results). Moral hazard predicts that agents will underinvest in costly activities (scope compliance, metric accuracy) when the principal cannot observe the effort. The guardrails and audit gates implement Holmstr\"{o}m's informativeness principle: git diffs (informative about actual changes), bus message patterns (informative about communication effort), and test results (informative about code quality) are all included in the agent evaluation contract. The graduated trust system adjusts the monitoring intensity based on observed moral hazard --- Gemini gets more scrutiny because it has demonstrated higher shirking rates.

entries.title: Governing the Commons: The Evolution of Institutions for Collective Action
entries.author: Ostrom, Elinor
entries.abstract: Ostrom demonstrates that communities can sustainably manage common-pool resources without either state regulation or privatization, through self-organized governance institutions. She identifies eight design principles for successful commons governance: clearly defined boundaries, congruence between rules and local conditions, collective-choice arrangements, monitoring, graduated sanctions, conflict-resolution mechanisms, minimal recognition of rights to organize, and nested enterprises for larger systems. The multi-agent coordination bus in hummbl-dev is a common-pool resource: all agents share write access, and overuse (spam, fabricated metrics, scope violations) degrades it for everyone. The bus governance architecture implements Ostrom's eight principles. Clearly defined boundaries: agent identity verification on bus messages. Congruence: per-agent guardrails adapted to each agent's behavior. Collective-choice: PROPOSAL messages enable agents to propose rule changes. Monitoring: the bus verifier and audit gates. Graduated sanctions: warnings, probation, scope restrictions, and eventual exclusion. Conflict resolution: the human operator as arbiter. Nested enterprises: the bus operates within the broader governance framework of frozen contracts and CI enforcement.

entries.title: A View of Cloud Computing
entries.author: Armbrust, Michael and Fox, Armando and Griffith, Rean and Joseph, Anthony D. and Katz, Randy and Konwinski, Andy and Lee, Gunho and Patterson, David and Rabkin, Ariel and Stoica, Ion and Zaharia, Matei
entries.abstract: Armbrust et al. define cloud computing as the intersection of utility computing (computing resources as a metered service) and SaaS (software delivered over the internet), and analyze the economic forces that make cloud computing viable: the illusion of infinite computing resources, elimination of up-front commitment, and pay-per-use pricing. They identify ten obstacles to cloud growth, including availability, data lock-in, and unpredictable performance. The economics of cloud computing directly inform the hummbl-dev cost governance architecture. The CostGovernorBridge enforces budget caps on API calls precisely because cloud and AI inference follow the utility pricing model Armbrust describes --- pay-per-use means costs can spike unpredictably. The circuit breaker pattern addresses the availability obstacle: when a cloud-hosted adapter fails, the breaker isolates the failure rather than cascading costs through retries. The GaaS revenue model is itself a cloud computing offering: governance primitives delivered as a metered service, implementing the utility computing paradigm for AI safety and compliance.

entries.title: The {API} Economy and Digital Transformation in Financial Services: The Case of Open Banking
entries.author: Zachariadis, Markos and Ozcan, Pinar
entries.abstract: Zachariadis and Ozcan analyze the API economy --- the ecosystem of value creation enabled by programmatic interfaces that allow organizations to expose their capabilities as composable services. In financial services, open banking APIs transformed the industry from vertically integrated institutions to horizontally composed service platforms, where fintech startups compose bank APIs into novel products. The API economy's core insight --- that value migrates from monolithic products to composable services --- describes the GaaS (Governance-as-a-Service) thesis precisely. The Base120 API and MCP server expose mental models as composable cognitive services. The delegation token API exposes authorization as a composable security service. The governance bus API exposes audit trails as a composable compliance service. Each hummbl-dev component is designed as an API-first building block that external builders can compose into their own agent architectures. The frozen contract schemas in contracts/ are the interface definitions that make this composition stable and predictable, implementing the API versioning discipline that the API economy demands.

entries.title: A Next-Generation Smart Contract and Decentralized Application Platform
entries.author: Buterin, Vitalik
entries.abstract: Buterin introduces Ethereum as a blockchain with a Turing-complete programming language that enables arbitrary smart contracts --- self-executing agreements where the terms are encoded in code and enforced by the network's consensus mechanism. The white paper describes how tokens (fungible and non-fungible) can represent ownership, access rights, and governance votes, creating programmable economic incentives. Token economics --- the design of incentive structures using cryptographic tokens --- has become a major subfield of mechanism design. The hummbl-dev delegation token system implements a centralized version of token economics. HMAC-SHA256 signed delegation tokens are capability tokens that grant bounded authority: specific actions, time-limited, chain-depth-constrained. While hummbl-dev tokens are not blockchain-based (they use centralized HMAC signing rather than distributed consensus), they implement the same economic primitives: capability delegation, authority attenuation, and revocation. The governance bus's append-only JSONL log serves the same transparency function as a blockchain ledger --- providing an immutable audit trail that all parties can verify, without the overhead of distributed consensus.
